meaning: emphasizes the word to which it is attached, with such meanings as 'precisely, quite, then'; e.g. kamāhu-mma 'precisely in that way, the very same'; wasta matakāftihu-mma 'upon his own shoulders'; yəəti-mma 'she herself'. It is also used with question particles: məntənu-mma 'what pray?, what then?', aytenu-mma 'where then?', əffo-mma 'how then?'. See also kəmma, mimma. For more details, see Dillmann 1907: 382.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: SAr. -m, -mw, -my, Hars. -me, Ug. -m, Akk. -ma. A. Ambros suggests, with reservations, Ar. -mma in 'allāhu-mma 'oh, Allah'. ETHIOPIC: Te. -mā, Tna. -əmmo, Amh. -mm 'and, even, also, too', Gaf. -m, Har. -ma (see Leslau, Mélanges Marcel Cohen [1970].263-73). Dillmann 1907:382 identifies it with the interrogative ma 'what?'. For other suggestions, see ib. n.1.

meaning: 'what?' (not often used); e.g. mi-lita walaka 'what have I to do with thee?', mi-əgabbaro 'what shall I do to him?', mi-matana 'how much?', migize 'when?', mi-ke 'how?' Any part of speech prefixed by -mi can express an exclamation; e.g. mi-bazhu 'how many they are!', mi-əddām 'how pleasant!'. See also Dillmann 1907:335.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar.Heb.Aram.Syr. mā, ma 'what?' (also used as an exclamation), Soq. mi 'when?', Heb.Ug.Ph. mi 'who?', Akk. mā 'what!' (with reservations, von Soden 570). See also Brockelmann 1908:326, Barth 1913:137, 140ff. For the connection between the interrogative and the exclamatory particle, see Jensen 1887:425. ETHIOPIC: Te. mə, mi 'what?', Gur. mə in məqar (< mə qar), lit. 'which thing?'. See also mannu, mənt, mimma.
perhaps reconstructed by K on the basis of Heb. mādad 'measure' (v).

meaning: (T) māˀədd, (Lt) maˀədd 'table, food, banquet, meal, dish (food), Eucharistic offering';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. māwida, mayda 'table' is rightly considered by Noeldeke 1910:54-5, and Jeffery 255 a Geez loanword as against Praetorius. ZDMG 61 (1907).622-3 who derives G. maˀədd from Ar. māwida. Littmann 1964:369 derives G. māˀədd from māˀəd-t < Ar. māwida. ETHIOPIC: Te. maˀəd 'table', Tna. maˀəddi, Amh. mad 'table, food', Gur. mad 'food'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. miəd 'table', Af. mādi, Or. māddi. As for Be. mēs connected by Reinisch 1895:173 with Bil. miəd, it is rather borrowed from an Arabic dialect (Oman, Iraq, and others) < Persian miz.

meaning: (M) 'twigs that hold up the straw of the roof';
origin_explanation: from ?gr: Gur. (Soddo) iggārā 'bind planks for building a house', magār 'planks for building a wall', Amh. magār 'wall of poles fastened into the ground', maggārā 'make a trellis', Har. migār āša 'tie pieces of wood against a wooden frame'. The root passed into Cushitic: Kam. meggaru-ta 'planks for building a wooden wall', Or. migara.
comes from Gk. (mnēsthēti) mou hagie '(remember me) oh, holy one!'; see also maˀagāyā, mukiryā, madāsuṭā (Hammerschmidt 1977:93).

meaning: 'get angry, take offense, scold, (T.M) threaten, be hostile, be ill-disposed'; taməwka, taməwka 'be threatened, be fright- ened', 'rough, wild'; (mə'wka 'uye 'he who fixes his eyes'); məw'k (K*), verbal noun of məwka; təməw'kt, təməkə't 'threat, anger, (K*) angry look'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: possibly Ar. ma'aka 'rub' (that is 'do something in a rough way'), ma'ik 'quarreler'.

meaning: a strange form; T has məkebe 'mule'.
'place in the middle, encircle, be in mid- course';
origin_explanation: denominative from mə'akal (for which see akala); also in Tna. mə'kälä 'be in the middle'.

meaning: 'gift, present, (T) pledge, vow, (KG) intercession, entreaty'; see 'ak'ata, and māk'tā.
Amh. čafrəgg 'low-grow- ing bush which is used like a brush in washing large crocks';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: perhaps Amh. mālä 'kind of grass or reed'.
ETHIOPIC, from Cushitic: Be. mano 'wolfhound'.
ETHIOPIC: Reinisch 1890:278 connects it with Sa. mayn 'Fussfläche'.

meaning: 'dish, plate, pan, basin, vessel used in offerings, (T.M) lid'; note that KG I has by error mə'afəs.
'skin, hide, leather, headcover, (T+) = Amh. dabba 'monk's mantle of yellow leather'; ma'asa (K*) 'remove the hide' (denominative);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ma'asa 'rub (leather) vigorously' (Dill- mann 196). The connection with Ar. ma'iz 'goat, goatskin' suggested by Barth 1893:62 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ma'si 'skin, leather'. Te. ma'as, Amh.Gur. mas, Har. Mās; also in Cushitic: Sa. ma'as 'tan hide'.

meaning: (T), name of the 12th Coptic month (corresponds to G. nahāse); see məsrā.
'hundred, century'; ma'ata (K*) 'be a hundred' (denominative); am'ata, (T) am'ata 'give a hundred, make a hundred'; təm'ət 'hundredfold';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. mi'a 'hundred' (on the various dialects, see Brockelmann 1908:487, Noeldeke 1910:152-3). SAr. m't, Soq. mi'e, Heb. me'ā, Syr. m(')ā, Md. ma, Ph.Ug. m't, Akk. me-atu. ETHIOPIC: Te. mə'ət 'hundred', Tna. mə'ti, Gur. māto.

meaning: 'when?'; mā'əze-nu, mā'əze-nu-mma 'when?'; 'aska mā'əze(nu) 'till when?, how long?'; 'əmmā'əze 'how long since?'; la-mā'əze 'for what time?'; 'i-mā'əze 'at no time, never';
origin_explanation: lit. 'what now? > when?'; from the interrogative m (see mi) and yə'əze 'now'. Littmann 1964:372 ana- lyzes mā'əze as mā-gizə 'what time?'. ETHIOPIC: Te. mā'əze, ma'əz 'when?', Tna. mā'az, mā'az, mā'aze, mā'əs.
the meaning 'divide' seems to be a denominative from mā'əzan 'angle'; cp. also Tna. (tə)māzənā 'divide'. The meaning 'hide' could go back to meaning 'put something in a corner'.
from Ar. mi'a 'oil of myrrh'; also Te. mi'a 'storax', Amh. mi'a.

meaning: 'be angry, rage, be enraged, indignant, be irritated'; am'a'əra, (Gr.107) am'a'əra 'irritate, provoke anger, provoke, inspire anger, enrage'; tam'a'əra 'be angry at one another'; 'astam'a'əra (Lt) 'provoke to anger'; 'astam'a'əra, caus. of tam'a'əra; also 'bear a grudge'; məw' 'angry, enraged, irritated, indignant'; ma'at, ma'at 'anger, wrath, rage, indignation, irksoməness, calamity, scourge, great number' (rəhuga ma'at 'long-suffering, forbearance, (rəhuqa ma'at 'long-suffering, forbearance, pa- tience'); təm'at 'anger, indignation';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: a noun developed from the root in Te.Tna. ma'at 'anger, rage, punishment', Gur. ma'at, mat 'rage, anger', Amh. mə'at 'calamity, scourge'. The root took the meaning of 'many, a lot' in Tna. mə'at, Amh. mat, G. ma'at. A de- nominative is formed in Tna. mə'atä 'flagellate' (tə)mā'a 'get angry', Gur. mə'ačä, Amh. mə'ačä. The connection with the noun for 'intestines' (heb- me-ayim) suggested by Praetorius 1890:26 is un- likely. The noun passed into Cushitic: Bil. mə'at 'God's punishment'. See also 'attama, ma'attom.

meaning: 'dash (waves), sweep across, cause waves to rise, inundate';
origin_explanation: denominative from mə'əbal 'wave', for which see 'abala.

meaning: 'counsel, warn, exhort, admonish'; tamə'ada, passive; tamə'ada 'counsel one another, exhort one another, warn one another'; ma'adī, act. part. of ma'ada; also 'advisor'; mə'ud (K*), pass. part. of ma'ada; mə'dət 'exhortation, admonition, advice'; mə'dān 'admonition, exhortation, religious in- struction, homily, discourse';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Soq. ma'ad 'intend' (for which see John- stone 1977:87). Among the various comparisons suggested by Dillmann 210 the root w'd (y'd) 'prom- ise' (with alternance m:w) is the most acceptable. ETHIOPIC: Te. ma'ada 'admonish, warn, advise'. Tna. ma'ada; Amh. mə'dən 'advice, counsel, exhor- tation' is taken from Geez.
from Ar. ma'din 'mineral, mine'; also in Amh. ma'əddən, Som. ma'din.
gives the impression of being a denominative of mā'ədot, above, but the meaning 'observe, watch' given by Y is strange, unless the original meaning is 'look across at something > observe'.

denominative from *mə'kəbit, metathesis of mə'kə- bit, for which see kəbaba, above. Note that 'amā'kə- bata quoted by Dillmann 868 occurs only in Job 42:10, while the Asmara Bible edition has 'amāk'a- ba, for which see mk'b, below.

meaning: (OT) 'water jug, laver'; in 2 Kings 8:8 quoted by OT 572 the noun is mä-äkökät, and not mä-äkät.
(K*) 'be haughty, commit an injustice, do a wrong, deny, dis-own'; 'am'ala (K*), causative; mam'ali, mam'äl (K*), part. of 'am'ala; a secondary form of m'äly, below. denominative from m'alä, below.
(Lt) 'spend the day' (used in greetings); denominative from m'alä, below.
(T) = Amh. doma 'digging tool with a wooden handle'; gives the impression of being an instrumental of *'lw.

'rebel, conspire, plot, (T) goad, urge on'; 'amä-laya (T.M), same; also causative; mam'aläy, mamä-aläy, mam'ali, mam'ali 'tyrant, usurper, rebel, one who subverts'; denominative from mä-älät, for which see 'alaya. See also m'alä I.
'fear, be frightened, regret'; metathesis of mamma'a, below.
origin_explanation: metathesis of mamma'a, below.

meaning: (T) = Amh. däggägä sälam 'a building to the left of the western entrance to a church where a meal is served for the clergy immediately after Mass, (KG) place where people assemble'.
(T) 'be muddy, be marshy, be slimy, (Gr.106) rot';
dung, (Sw. in D 120, mud, mire, marsh, marshland'.
(Lt) 'food, rich sweets'; Is strange in comparison with ma'anä, Could it be identified with ma'ant, be inserted b?
origin_explanation: Is strange in comparison with ma'anä, Could it be identified with ma'ant, be inserted b?

meaning: (Sw.in D 991,1) 'small pitcher used by you fetching water'.
'give in safekeeping, deposit, entrust, watch over'; mä'qäbännä 'pledge, guardianship, protection'; (mä'qäbännä näwäy 'deposit'); the verb is a denominative from mä'äqäb, see 'aqäba.
origin_explanation: the verb is a denominative from mä'äqäb, see 'aqäba.

meaning: 'gather, collect, (KG) dip'; denominative from mä'qäl; see 'aqala.
origin_explanation: denominative from mä'qäl; see 'aqala.

meaning: 'honey, honey-comb'; (ma'ära gadäm, ma'ära sadanä 'wild honey'); (ma'ära gärä 'wax', lit. 'sweetness of the comb'); tam'ara (Lt) 'be filled with scent'; 'am'ara 'sweeten with honey'; SEMITIC: the connection with Heb. ya'ar 'honey-comb' suggested by Dillmann 207 (and taken up by Appleyard 29) is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. mä'ar 'honey', Amh. mar. Har. mär. Gur. ma'ar. For ma'ära sadanä, cp. Tna. säde-nay mä'ar. See also m'rr, m'rr.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the connection with Heb. ya'ar 'honey-comb' suggested by Dillmann 207 (and taken up by Appleyard 29) is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. mä'ar 'honey', Amh. mar. Har. mär. Gur. ma'ar. For ma'ära sadanä, cp. Tna. säde-nay mä'ar. See also m'rr, m'rr.

meaning: 'moment, time'; ma'ra 'once, at once, at one time, a while, for the present, just now, presently'; ma'ra dägmä 'another time'; ma'ra ... ma'ra 'at one time... at another time'; wam'ära ... wam'ära 'one time... and another time'; bam'är 'in a moment, once, all at once, suddenly, in an instant'; bazäntu ma'r 'this once'; lam'är 'for a moment'; lavänta ma'r 'in a moment'; hədäjätä ma'ra, ma'ra hədäta 'for a little time'; 'ammär lam'är 'now and then, separately'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the connection with Ar. marra 'time, turn' suggested by Dillmann 206 is doubtful. ETHIOPIC: probably to be identified with Cushitic: Sa.Af. mär 'spend the time', Som. mar 'moment'.

meaning: 'earthenware tripod on which a cooking pot rests above the fire'; see also mörä (under mar'a).
'measure of cereal'; ETHIOPIC: Tna. mä'ro, same.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. mä'ro, same.

meaning: 'become sweet like honey'; 'am'ar'ara, (Gr.106) 'am'ar'ara 'sweeten, sweet, find pleasure'; ma'ar'ar (fem. ma'ar'är) 'sweet, sweet-tasting, honey-sweet'; ma'ar'ar (T) 'perfume'; ma'ar'ärä (Gr) 'sweetness of honey'; ma'ar'arännä 'sweetness'; from ma'ar, above, with reduplication of the two radicals. See also m'rr.
origin_explanation: from ma'ar, above, with reduplication of the two radicals. See also m'rr.

meaning: 'est ainsi dénommé 'chapitre' (sous-entendu du Deggua), parce qu'à l'opposé des autres livres de chant il ne peut être employé seul: le recours au Deggua étant absolument indispensable et réciproquement le Deggua pouvant être utilisé sans le recours au ma'räf (Velat 50, 679ff). See 'rf I.
'cover, cover with a veil'; mä'ärät (T) 'head cover'; ETHIOPIC: perhaps Te. mä'rät 'treasure', that is, 'something hidden or covered', or would it be a misprint for mä'äfärt (see 'afara)?
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: perhaps Te. mä'rät 'treasure', that is, 'something hidden or covered', or would it be a misprint for mä'äfärt (see 'afara)?

meaning: (T) 'equalize, arrange, do well'; denominative from *m'räy, from 'araya; also Tna. ma'raya 'equalize'.
origin_explanation: denominative from *m'räy, from 'araya; also Tna. ma'raya 'equalize'.

meaning: 'prophesy, foretell, predict'; undoubtedly in connection with m'ry, below, augmented by '.
origin_explanation: undoubtedly in connection with m'ry, below, augmented by '.

meaning: = Amh. baläm"al honä 'be a favorite', translated wrongly in MA 92 'être habile, adroit, capable' as if it were baläm"aya (balämuya). In view of Amh. masä 'be clever, be cunning', the Amharic translation of tamo'asa should have been baläm"aya 'skillful, handy'.

'be a widow, be unmarried, be divorced' (perhaps a denominative); tamä'saba, same; 'amä'saba = Amh. fätta 'divorce' (not 'open' as given in H 580); mä'ässab, mä'ässab, mä'ässabät, (T) mä'ässabät (pl. mä'ässab, mä'ässabän, mä'ässabät) 'unmarried, husbandless, widow, widower'; mä'ässabännä 'widowhood, celibacy, state of being a divorcée'; SEMITIC: Dillmann 973 (followed by Delitzsch 29, von Soden 267) connects it with Sem. zb 'leave, abandon'; cp. also Ar. 'azaba 'live in celibacy'. Brockelmann 1908:163 thinks that G. 'sb is the original form and has become zb in the other Semitic languages by assimilation.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 973 (followed by Delitzsch 29, von Soden 267) connects it with Sem. zb 'leave, abandon'; cp. also Ar. 'azaba 'live in celibacy'. Brockelmann 1908:163 thinks that G. 'sb is the original form and has become zb in the other Semitic languages by assimilation.

meaning: 'make the sign of the cross, cross oneself, put a cord around the neck indicating adherence to Christianity, seal'; tamā-taba (M), passive;
origin_explanation: denominative from mā'əzər; see 'zr.

meaning: (Lt), amabkəha (MA) 'blow on a fire to activate it'; see bak"ha.
'rule, domineer, exercise ty, wield power, (Sw. in D 183) beha gantly'; 'amabbalala, causative;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Dillmann 182 considers it a de from *mbl < b*l (see ba*la I, above) > Praetorius 1890:30.

meaning: 'woman who has no husband (often a woman who does not remarry becomes a nun), widow, poor (for 'nun' and 'poor', cp. Te. mänkäśä 'be po mänäk'se 'monk'), (T.Sw. in D 486) d promiscuous' (a widow deprived of her liveli hood sometimes becomes a prostitute), mabāllənnā 'widowhood';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. mäbäl 'widow', mäbäl 'wido mänbäla, mänbäla, mäbäla 'remain a w Tna. mäbällät 'widow, nun'. Amh. mäbäla widow who lives alone', əmmäbällät 'wido alone'. Dillmann 486 derives it from a non-existing root bll, but G.Tna. and Amh. could better be explained through bll (see balata) 'separate, render lonely'.

meaning: (T.M) 'dig' (denominative from mabāro); mabāro (T.M) 'means of digging (hoe, spade)';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. mäbaro 'hoe', from baräwä 'dig' passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af. mabāro 'hoe'. The deriva tion from the root br (Arabic) 'dig a well' suggested by Reinisch 1890:257 is unlikely.
has the appearance of an Amharic form from bärra 'shine'.
borrowed from Amharic where the noun is possibly a dialectal variant of mado(t).

meaning: (MA) 'build a wall (of stones or bricks)'; madab 'base, socle, projection, wall, (T.M) ele vated place, seat, stool, platform';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. mädäb 'raised part of the floor for sitting or sleeping', Gur. mädäb, Arg. mädäb 'sleeping place made of masonry', Har. mädäb 'raised seat', Amh. mädäb 'stall for merchandise, bench of stones and mud built against the inside wall of the house'; passed into Cushitic: Kam.Had. mädäba, Or. madabi.
denominative from madbal (see dabala); also in Amh. mädäbbälä 'gather'.

meaning: (T) 'leather thong used in fastening loads to the backs of beasts of burden';
origin_explanation: from Amh. mädäbal 'packing strap or rope'.

meaning: (KBT 123) 'place where many people gather, place where assemblage of objects is arranged';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. mädäbara, same, from däbbärä 'heap up', Tna. däbärä. G. Salinger suggests a bor rowing from Ar. mudabbar 'arranged'.
transcription of Heb.Aram. midbār.
from Gk. madbaritis 'Desert Region', from Heb. (Aram) midbār.

meaning: (Sw. in D 1394) 'hit, (Gr) exe cute, (K*) spread, level'; madäd (Gr) 'executioner'; madäd 'place of execution, place of cor poral punishment'; the basic meaning is perhaps 'spread, level' > 'flatten by cutting down the uneven places > hit, destroy, execute' (for the passage of meanings, cp. Ar. basata 'spread', Iraqi Ar. busat 'flagellate', sug gestion made by Ambros).
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. madda (mdd) 'spread', Ar. mudd 'measure' (n), Sar. md-t 'period of time', Heb. mā dad 'measure', (hit)moded 'stretch out upon', Ug. mdd 'measure', Akk. madādu. ETHIOPIC: Amh. mädämmädä (reduplicated root mdd) 'level, flatten, cut down one after another (trees), destroy', Tna. mädämmädä 'level, flatten', Te. mädäda (mdd) 'spread, stretch, attack'.
from Ar. midfa' (for which see Ayalon, Index, p. 153); also in Tna. mädəfəṣ, Amh. mädəf.

meaning: (D 1394), for madāməḥt, madāməḥt, for which see damḥa II.
(one would expect madha) (T.Y.M) madha 'grind grain, scrape clean';
origin_explanation: denominative from madhe; see daḥaya.
SEMITIC: Ar. dalham 'dark', idlahama 'become dark'. W. Müller suggests a borrowing from Ar. muzallimā, dialectally mudallimā 'dark'.
tamadlawə (T) 'be hypocritical'; denominative from *madlot, madlawi; see dalawa II.
denominative from *madlot, madlawi; see dalawa II.
from Ar. madīna 'capital city'; also Tna.Amh. mädi-na.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. medaq, maydaq 'small gazelle'. Amh. midaqq 'a gazelle'.
perhaps denominative from madqəḥ 'awl, hammer', and 'amadqəḥa would then mean 'make hammers, awls, and so on'; see daqha I.
denominative from madqəḥ; see daqḥa II.

meaning: 'earth, ground, soil, field, country, land, territo-ry, district, region, floor (of a house), bottom (of a pit)'
origin_explanation: mədrāwi 'earthly, worldly, of earth, terrestrial being'.

meaning: 'be wide, be long. (Lt) be stretched out, be extended, be expanded' (de-nominative)
origin_explanation: Madara (Gr.112) 'create the earth' (denomina-tive); tamadara (Lt), same as madara; SEMITIC: Ar. madar 'clods of earth, mud', Sar. mdr 'territory, ground', Mh. medər 'sun-dried brick', PbH. medər 'clod of earth', Syr. medrā 'clod, soil'. Note that while von Soden 651 renders Akk. madru by 'eine Art Land' and compares it with G.

meaning: states 'meaning uncertain'. As for midirtu 'Gartenbeet' (von Soden 650), CAD 10, 2, p.48 gives a variant mitirtu 'a type of field or orchard, characterized by a special irrigation system'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te.Amh.Gur. mədər 'soil, earth'. Tna. mədri. See also madrawa, əm(m)ədəru.
see also dargu.
'amadrawa (K*.MA), causative; denominative from mədr.

meaning: 'pertaining to flame, epithet of Christ, (T) cry of woe'
origin_explanation: comes from Gk. (mnēstēthēti) mou despota (remem-ber me) oh, Lord!'; see also muwäqäyä, mukiryä.

meaning: (K*) 'gar-ment, shirt, (K) outer garment, honorific gar-ment', (KG) = Amh. čufa 'gold or silver bracelet given warriors to wear on the arm as a military decoration'
origin_explanation: transcription of Heb. mad 'cloth, garment'.
one wonders whether there is not some confusion in the Amharic renderings čufa and čäffe for madayo (see Mädäy, above).
madyäm; see *dym.
modä; see mws II.
mdd, madda, madada (yəmdəd)

meaning: (T.Y.K*) 'ferment, be sour', umdada (Gr.118), causative; madəd (Gr.119) 'fermented, sour'; madəd, fem. maddäd, (T) masiṣ 'acid, sour, (T) angry, ill-tempered, rapacious'; madəd, (Lt) mədäd 'vinegar'; maddat, verbal noun of madda;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. madad 'sour milk', Heb. maṣṣā 'un-leavened bread'. Tna. mäṣäṣä 'be acid, be sour', mäṣiṣ 'vinegar'. Te. mäṣṣa 'be acid, be sour', Amh. läṭäṭä, mäṣäṣä 'turn sour'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. maṭaṭä 'vinegar'. See also maṣawa.
see məṣiṣit.
mawädafət)
'amodafa (Gr.119), causative; tamodafa (Gr.119), passive; tamawädafa (Gr.119) 'throw at one another with slings'; modafi (Gr.119), act. part. of modafa; denominative from modaf, above.
(note the misprint mərärəhi M 113).

meaning: 'season of flowers, spring, season after the rains (September-December)'
origin_explanation: madawa, maṣawa (T.Y) 'pass the season madaw'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. mäṣäw 'season of flowers' (which see Coulbeaux-Schreiber 194), Amh. mäṣäw 'season after the summer rains' (from Geez); also Cushitic: Bil. maṣuw, Qem. mäṣwa, Qab. maṣw. Or. əmiṣa. The Cushitic forms were borrowed by Har. əmiš 'harvest', Gur. mäṣä, name of the fourth month. On the confusion in the terminology con-cerning the seasons of the year, see hagäy in Dill-mann 130.
misprint for mofara, denominative from mof 'beam of the plough' (see wəfara I).
ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. mäfläs 'wild boar'. Hommel 385

meaning: gives it an original meaning 'umher schweifend, wild', deriving it undoubtedly from falasa.
'steer, be a steward, direct, manage, administer, be in charge of, maintain, surround to protect, protect, (K*) feed'
origin_explanation: 'amaggaba 'appoint a guardian, appoint an administrator or an overseer, place in charge of'; tamaggaba, pass. of maggaba; maggäbi 'administrator, guardian, steward, manager, prior (of a community), purveyor, one who is in charge, officer, prefect'; (maggäbe hamar 'captain of a ship'); maggub, pass. part. of maggaba; magb. (Gr.114) magb 'guardianship, steward-ship, tutelage, management, ministry, adminis-tration, office, function, duty'; magbat, verbal noun of maggaba; magbot 'mandate, administration, tutelage'; magbənnā. (Gr.114) magbənnā 'administration, management, guardianship, mandate'; 'amaggäbi. part. of 'amaggaba; also 'guard-ian'; 'amaggabo 'mandate'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. mäggäbi 'feed', mäggäbi 'steward, manager', Amh. mäggäbi 'feed', mäggäbi 'house-keeper, steward', Gur. (Soddo) mägb 'food'. The connection with Te. mägba 'be in the middle' sug-gested by Littmann-Höfner 142 (followed by D. Cohen 1976:105 who connects it with the root gwb) is unlikely since G. maggaba does not mean 'surround (umgeben)', but 'surround to protect'. See also maggäbiṭ.

ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. mäggäbiṭ, Gur. mäggäbiṭ. Conti Rossini 1923:376 rightly connects it with mgb (see maggaba, above), that is, 'the month that feeds'. The noun passed into Cushitic: Kam. mäg-gäbiṭä, Had. mäggäwiṭe.

meaning: 'wave, flood, tempest', (mawāgaddā wāsāt [T] 'blast of hot air'), (mawāgaddā karamt [T] 'driving rain, tempest'); (amogaddā 'make waves, cause to rise in waves'; tamogaddā (K*) 'passive of 'amogaddā')
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. mawğa(t) 'wave', Mh. mowğūṭ. Buhl 404, Koehler 501, and Baumgartner 526 connect it with Heb. mwg (nīf'al) 'wave to and fro, swerve'. As for G. mogad, Praetorius, ZDMG 47 (1893) 392 derives it from Ar. mawğa(t) through Somali where final t becomes d. However, mogad could be a variant of mogat within Geez by t becoming d through assimila-tion. Noeldeke 1910:198, n.1 derives, with reserva-tions, G. mogad from Ar. waqāda "dessen Grundbe-deutung etwa 'stossen' sein könnte" and he does not agree with the opinion expressed by Praetorius. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. mogād 'wave'; Amh. māwğ 'wave', Te. mog come from Ar. mawğ. See also mogat, below.

meaning: 'ignite, kindle'. Te. mogada 'fire'. Amh. mogādā 'use as fuel, put in the fire', magādo 'firewood'. Har. māgāda 'burn, set on fire', Gur. magādā 'kindle, burn, light a fire'.
origin_explanation: It is perhaps to be connected with Tna. wāg'ādā 'start a fire', mawag'ādī 'firewood'; in this case the verbs with initial m of the various languages would be denominatives from *mgd 'firewood'. In Leslau 1988: 241 I expressed the opinion (partially following Praeto-rius 1879:175) that Ethiopic *mag(g)ada, mogada is perhaps a denominative from magādo 'firewood' from a root *wgd that could be compared with Heb. yāqad 'burn', Ar. waqāda (so also Cerulli 1936:261 in connec-tion with Har. māgāda).

meaning: 'look from the corner of the eye'; denominative from magdam; see gadama.
origin_explanation: denominative from magdam; see gadama.
see gaddama.

meaning: 'illuminate, light up'; possibly for 'amegha, a denominative from a non-attested mgh in Geez, but occurring in Tna. as mægh 'dawn, early morning', coming from wāghe 'to dawn'.
origin_explanation: possibly for 'amegha, a denominative from a non-attested mgh in Geez, but occurring in Tna. as mægh 'dawn, early morning', coming from wāghe 'to dawn'.

phonetics: Tna. mag"aya, mag"aya, Te. mogya, Amh.Gur. muğa
meaning: 'couch grass', 'a sort of corn', 'wild panic grass'
origin_explanation: also in Cushitic: Qem. muğa. G. muğğā. The noun is not to be derived from a non-attested g"hy as suggested by Dillmann 1146. Note that Dillmann 1907:277 derives G. mæg"ahyā 'weed', 'that which makes waste' from Ar. ğāhiya 'be ruined (house), widen (a wound)'. See also muğğayāhu, below.

meaning: 'fester, be purulent'; 'make pus come out'; mugl 'pus, blister, mark of a blow'; mamgal (Gr.450) 'pus'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. magala 'be blistered', Ar. mugl 'pus', Aram.-Syr. muglā. Note that 2004 and Brockelmann 1928:37 record (Byzantine) Gk. moukhla in connection with Syr. muglā. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Gur. māgālā 'suppurate', Amh. māggālā, Har. māgālā 'suppurate', Arg. māgal; passed into Cushitic: Bil. mogla 'pus'.

meaning: = Amh. ambiyagusəm 'chest strap for a mount, breastplate attached to the saddle'

origin_explanation: probably connected with mənkup, below.
see ganapa, and maguppā, below.
SEMITIC: related to Ar. qanaba 'trim (a vine)'.
from Gk. magnētis 'magnet'; also Ar. magnāṭīs. See also maglīṭos, māgnīṭos, mang"anṭīs.
see mag"anpā, above.
see wagara.

meaning: = Amh. ğānfo 'iron counterbalance on the butt of a spear'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. māg"rāb.

meaning: 'be gracious'; amogasa 'bestow grace, favor, (Gr.113) make someone a favorite'; tamogasa 'be given grace, be gracious, be favorite, find favor with others'; mogas 'favor, grace, graciousness, charm, love-liness, benevolence, dignity'; (mogasa qāl 'gracious words'); mogus (Gr) 'full of grace'; mogasāwī 'graceful, gracious, charming (Gr.114) favorite, friendly, agreeable'; mamog (g)as, (Lt) mamog (g)asī 'well-disposed, benevolent'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh. mogas 'benevolence, kindness, favor', Amh. moggasā 'be in favor, be honored'.

meaning: 'blame, reproach, rebuke, irritate, utter harsh words, attack with harsh words, be harsh, degrade'; (H 235 = Amh. aqqallalā meaning in this context 'degrade', not 'make small'); tamogada, (Gr.115) tamag(g)asa, passive; 'insult, degrade' (H 230 = Amh. nāṭṭābā meaning 'insult' in this context, not 'drip'); 'amg"asa, (T.M) 'amag"asa, 'amag"ada, same as mag"asa; also causative'
origin_explanation: perhaps a denominative from *mg"s, from a root wgs, for which cp. Amh. wāggāṭā 'upset, harass, trouble'.
see mogad, above.
see g"aṭaya.
denominative from magz'a, for which see gaz'a.
gives the impression of a plural form.

meaning: 'a grass that grows in the rainy season in soft soil'
origin_explanation: seems to be a misprint of mag"ahyā p. above. For the frequent ending -hu, see Tayyā 92.
see hababa II.
perhaps māhbaba, denominative from māhbab (see hababa II) with a generalized meaning 'work' instead of the specific meaning 'press grapes', as expressed by hababa II.

is either a secondary form of *māhyaba, māhyaba, below, or the Amh. ṭāllāqā is placed here by error, for Amh. ṭāllāqā given one line below as transla-tion for māhyaba (p. 328), and māhbaba may then have a meaning not given by Y.
see hababa II.
see māhəbat.
see haddama.

meaning: 'have regard for, pity, have pity, treat with consideration, be compassionate, be sparing, spare, take care of, save, refrain, withhold, (Y) estimate, ap-praise' (could possibly go back to the mean-ing 'spare'); 'amhaka, (Gr.79) amhaka, causative; tamahaka 'be treated with consideration'; mahāki (K*) 'one who is treated with consid-eration, one who obtains pardon, one who spares'; mahik, mahikot 'clemency, pity, compassion, indulgence, sparing'; (zə'ənbālaməhik 'unsparingly, mercilessly, ruth-lessly'); mahkat, same as məhik;
'soften a thing', 'Ug. mhk 'care, worry' (so also Fisher 123).
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. mahaka 'soften a thing'. ETHIOPIC: Te. mahaka 'spare, have pity'.
Dillmann 10 derives it from a root *hkw which he compares with hwk, hkk. In this case the verb would be a denominative from a noun such as *mahkaw.

meaning: 'teach, instruct, educate, train, discipline'; 'amahara (Gr.71), 'amahra (K*), causative; tamahara, tamahra 'be taught, be instructed, learn, be skilled, be versed in, be disciplined'; mahari 'teacher, tutor, title of a learned man'; mahur 'taught, instructed, trained, learned, skilled, experienced, expert, versed (in)'; mah(a)r (Lt) 'teaching, doctrine'; maharo, mahirot, same; 'astamharo 'teaching' (Velat 32, n. 5); mamhar 'teacher, instructor, master, superior, abbot, prior of a monastery'; (mamhara 'orit 'doctor of the law', that is, the Octateuch); ('afa mamhar 'representative of an abbot'); mamhar (fem. of mamhar) 'abbess'; məmhar (instead of mahmar, Gr.72) 'place of learning, school'; mamharənnä (Gr.72) 'title of doctor'; tamahari 'disciple, student'; tamahar 'instruction, teaching, study'; tamhari 'teaching, study, doctrine, discipline'; təmn(ə)hart 'teaching, study, learning, educa-tion, erudition, doctrine, dogma, method, disci-pline, science, ability, skill'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. mahara 'be skillful', SAR. t-mhr-t 'contingent of Bedouin mercenaries', Heb. mahir 'quick, skilled' (Ludolf 638; so also Ullendorff, VT 6[1965],195), Aram. mah(y)r 'capable, skillful', Syr. mahira 'trained, skillful', Ug. mhr 'serviceman' (Gor-don 431). Note that the meaning 'quick, fast' also occurs in Aram. (Palmyra), Syr. mahira 'promptly, skillfully'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. mahara 'teach', Te. mahara, Amh.Gur. (ta)mara 'study', astamara '(am)mahera 'study'.

meaning: 'take as booty, plunder, loot, rob, pillage'; 'aməhraka, pass. of mahraka; mahraki (Gr.73), act. part. of mahraka; mahruk (Gr.73), pass. part. of mahraka; maharka 'booty, spoils, plunder'; maharkənnä, same;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. mhrk 'booty' (W. Müller, ZDMG 1981,401). ETHIOPIC: Te. maraka, Tna. mara-ka, marraka, Arg. marraka, Gur. maraka, Har. maraka. Note that the root is mhrk in Geez while in the other Ethiopian languages the root seems to be a triradical mrk in the C-type. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Aw. marex"a, Kham. mirek, Qem. mirak.

meaning: 'utensil of glass, glass cup, bottle, crystal, glass';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: one might be inclined to connect with Ar. maha(i) 'crystal', as suggested by Noel-deke 1910:170 (following Gesenius), but while Ar. ma-ha(i) could be derived from the root for 'water' (see mahwa, below), this does not seem to be the case for this noun in the various Ethiopian languages. ETHIOPIC: Te. maho 'goblet', Amh. maho, mo (also from Geez), Gur. (Zway) mawa; passed into Cushitic: Qem. mo 'glass, cup'. The identification of 'maha 'water' with Amh. maho 'cup, carafe', G. mahaw, as suggested by Praetorius 1879:54, is unlikely. Leslau 1963:106 suggests a connection with Har. mahawa 'object, goods, utensil'.

meaning: 'melt (tr), liquefy, dissolve, make dwindle'; 'amhaw 'melt (tr), liquefy, dissolve dwindle'.
origin_explanation: tamahwa, same as mahwa; mahaw, fem. mahut (Lt) 'melted, fluid'; mahaw (Gr.73) verbal noun of mahwa; mahaw (Gr.73) 'place where one liquefies'. SEMITIC: it is tempting to connect this root with mahwa 'be watery (milk)'; see Dillmann 145, Noel-deke 1910:170, and others. However, while Ar. huwa can be derived from the root for 'water' m(a) enlarged by h, as suggested by Noeldeke this procedure would not apply to G. mahwa the enlargement of a biradical root through h does not occur; cp. perhaps Ar. (Yem) mihi 'be (W. Müller). ETHIOPIC: Te. maha 'melt, be melted', Amh. ala, with reduplication of the root in ma-m'amm'a (passed into Or. mamawa), Har. ma-Gur. (Zway) mamu 'be melted (butter, honperhaps also Cushitic: Be mu 'humidity'.

meaning: 'rub, massage'; for mahwa, and see mahawa, below.
'hidden, concubine'; from Ar. mahzan 'storeroom, storehouse', that is, 'place where one keeps (hides) things'; also in makzan 'storeroom'.

'entrust, give in deposit, commit someone's care, hand over into the care of, under protection, commend, recommend, con-fide'; tamahdana 'receive in trust, seek protection, take refuge, take sanctuary, put trust in, trust, place oneself under protection, be protected, appeal for protection'; mahdən, pass. part. of 'amahdana; also 'de-posited, entrusted'; mahdənna 'deposit, trust'; mahdən, mahdant, (T) mahdant 'trust, de-posit, pledge, custody'; mahdənət 'deposit'; 'amahdani 'depositor'; mamhədən 'pledge, refuge'.
origin_explanation: mamhadan, act. part. of 'amahdana; mamhadan, mamhadan 'place of refuge'; tamahdani 'one who receives in trust, trustee'. SEMITIC: Sar. mhdn 'favor, grant'. ETHIOPIC: the verb is a denominative from mahadan 'bosom' (see hadana, above), in Tna. (ta)mahsa 'place oneself under protection, appeal for protec-tion, seek protection, take refuge', Te. (ta)maha-dana, Amh. (ta)mattana.

meaning: 'weed'; metathesis in relation to msg"ahya, above.
'think, have in mind'; comes close to mahaka (mahaka) 'have regard for'.

'swear, take an oath'; (mahala ba-hassat, mahala ba-'amada 'perjure oneself, swear falsely'); 'amhala 'adjure, compel to swear'; tamahla, pass. of mahala; tamahala 'swear to one another, conspire, form a conspiracy, bind one another by an oath, make a covenant'; mahali, act. part. of mahala; mahul (Gr.78, sic!) 'one who swears'; 'amhali, part. of 'amhala; mahala, mahala 'oath, adjuration, treaty, cove-nant'; (mahala ba-hassat or za-hassat 'perjury'); (bal'a mahala 'violate an oath');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 155 identifies it with Ar. mahalla 'plot, intrigue, scheme'; cp. also SAR. mhly-m 'oath' (?), Heb. mahal 'forgive', the forgiving possibly expressed by an oath. ETHIOPIC: Tna. mahala 'swear', Te. mahala, Amh.Gur. mala, Arg. mahala.

meaning: mahlāla (K*) mahlal 'supplication, invocation, solemn service, adjuration, imprecation'; mahallāla bamalaw 'imprecation at full length'; ma-həllā bag 'adalo 'abridged imprecation' (from ma-ba, g'adala; see Velat 577ff); mahallāla, mahallā 'same; also 'intercession, advo-cacy';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Praetorius 1890:369-70 thinks that the correct form is (ta)māhlala (with h) and not (ta)-mālala (with h) and considers it a denominative from *mḥll to be derived from Semitic hll 'praise' (Ar.Heb. Syr), so also Christian, WZKM 55 (1959). 169. How-ever, in view of the more frequent form with h the verb is (ta)māhlala even though Te. and Tna. have the root mḥll, but also mḥll (see below). The root mḥll is perhaps to be identified with mḥl 'swear' (see mahala, above) with the reduplication of the last radical. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tä)māhlalä, (tä)māhlalä 'invoke God, pray', Te. (tä)māhlala, Amh.Gur. (tä)māllalä, Arg. (tä)māllalä 'beg'. The root passed into Cushit-ic: Bil. mahlal 'implore God'.
see ḥalaya.

meaning: (Gr.73) 'scrub, rub, soften a hide'; 'amḥana (Gr.73), causative; tamahana (Gr.73), passive; maḥāni (Gr.73), act. part. of maḥana; maḥun (Gr.73), pass. part. of maḥana; maḥān, maḥān 'soft hide', possibly 'skin dyed red'; maḥāne, məḥnat (Gr.73) 'act of softening the hide'; məmḥān (Gr.73) 'place where one softens hide';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. maḥana 'soften the hide'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. maḥanä 'pound, crush'.

meaning: 'pay homage, make a gift, bow down to greet' (denominative); maḥanā, maḥanā, maḥanā, manā 'pre-sent, gift, tribute, offering, sacrifice';
origin_explanation: from Gk. manaa, transcription of Heb. minḥā 'gift'.

meaning: 'shuttle, length of the warp, weaver's reed';

origin_explanation: Ar. muharram.

meaning: 'have compassion, be compassionate, show mercy, have mercy, have pity, pardon'; tamaharra, tamahara 'obtain mercy, be shown mercy or pity'; *astamhara 'implore pity, crave mercy, seek mercy, ask for mercy, be prone to pity, have pity, intercede, be merciful, be compassionate, obtain pardon'; *astamhara 'ask for mercy from one another'; maḥāri 'merciful, compassionate, gra-cious, one who has pity'; maḥur 'one who obtains mercy'; maḥrat 'compassion, pardon, mercy, pity, clemen-cy, affection'; (amäta maḥrat 'year of grace'; it des-ignates the Ethiopian calendar); mamḥar (Lt) 'one who implores com-passion, worthy of compassion'; *astamharo (Gt) 'characters of the dagg™ä-anti-phonary, (Gr.78) supplication (religious service)'; mastamḥar (instead of mastamḥar of D), part. of *astamhara;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: metathesis in Ar. raḥima 'have mercy, be merciful', Sar. rḥm, Heb. raḥam 'love, have compas-sion', Aram.-Syr. rḥem, Ug. rḥm, Akk. rēmu; consid-ered a denominative from rḥm 'womb'. Ar. raḥim, Heb. rāḥam, Aram. raḥmā, Syr. raḥmā, Akk. rēmu. Amh.Gur. Marā. Note that Te. rāḥama 'have pity on', rāḥamä are taken from Arabic. Praetorius 1890:21 derives the root mḥr from G. ḥora (adding unglaublich dies auf den ersten Blick scheint'). SAr. ḥwr 'go, return > answer', SAr. ḥwr 'be gracious', Ethiopic mḥr would then be a denominative from mḥr 'gnädige Antwort, Gnade'. This deriva-tion is not convincing.

meaning: (instead of ma-hurəḥwāt of T.M); see ḥwr.
'extirpate, eradicate, uproot, rake, pull out'; maḥus, pass. participle;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. maḥaśa 'knead, rub (leather)'; possibly Ar. maša (with metathesis) 'wipe out, erase', Mh. maḥōš 'wipe'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. maḥaśä 'weed, extirpate, pull out', Amh.Gur. masā 'dig out, pluck out, excavate'.
SEMITIC: Getatchew suggests, with reservations, the possibility of an original meaning 'dig out' (see ma-sa I) in the sense of softening the earth around a growing plant, and thus making it grow.

meaning: (D 95), māḥaśa (L) 'suckling kid, lamb, young goat'; (māḥaśa tali 'young kid'); (māḥaśa bagga 'suckling lamb, calf, kid'); tamāḥaśa'a (Lt) 'represent oneself as a lamb' denominative);
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. maḥsi, maḥəśä 'kid'.

meaning: 'smite, cut, pierce through (T.Y.M) split, chisel, destroy, injure, insult, blame, criticize'; tamāḥaśa (K*), passive; maḥāśi, act. part. of maḥaśa; maḥuś, pass. part. of maḥaśa; maḥśat, verbal noun of maḥaśa;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. maḥaśa 'stamp, trample'; related to Mh. mḥd 'smite, hew out, defeat', Soq. meḥad 'weave', Heb. maḥaś 'strike, break to pieces' (also 'churn' according to Dahood 1968:146), Aram. mḥa (< ma-ḥa', mḥd), Md. mḥa, Ug. mḥs 'smite, strike', Akk. maḥāṣu 'hit, strike, weave'. See on this root M. Held, JAOS 79 (1959).169-176. See also maḥaśa. ETHIOPIC: Tna. maḥaśä 'hit, cut, break', Te. maḥaśa 'churn, shake milk', Arg. māḥaṭa 'hit', Har. māḥaṭa, Gaf. maṣā, Gur. māṭā.

meaning: 'flee, emigrate, set completely (sun), disappear, (Y) be teary (eye)'; M 104 ren-ders it 'intrude, invade' apparently mistaking Amh. təqəbəqəq alä 'set completely (sun)', Tayyä's translation of maḥaśa, for təqəbəqəq alä 'meddle'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: from hsy: Heb. ḥāṣā (ḥsy) 'divide', Syr. ḥaṣā (ḥsy), Ph. ḥṣy 'half', Akk. eṣu 'slit'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. maḥaśä 'axe', Te. mäḥazä (d'Abbadie), Amh. maḥṣe (from Geez), maṭṭe. The root is related to ḥaṣaṣa, above.

meaning: 'uproot, pluck out, pull out, tear away, tear out, carry away. (T.Y) rub'; amḥawa (Gr.79), causative; tamahawa, pass. of maḥawa; maḥāwi (Gr.79), act. part. of maḥawa; maḥāw, fem. maḥut (Gr.79), pass. part. of maḥa-wa; maḥwat, verbal noun of maḥawa; məmḥāw (Gr.80), mamḥo (Gr.80) 'place where one uproots, instrument for uprooting';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. maḥā (mḥw/y) 'erase, cancel', Heb. māḥā (mḥy) 'wipe out, exterminate', Aram. məḥā 'efface', Ph. mḥy, Ug. mḥy (Aistleitner, no. 1540) 'be effaced'. Akk. maḥu, 'etwa "wegstossen"' (?) suggested by von Soden 637 is doubtful. ETHIOPIC: Tna. mäḥawä 'uproot'.

meaning: 'draw water with a large container, dip, plunge'; denominative from mäḥyab; see ḥyb.
'be-come a young man, become a lover, be flirta-tious'; tamāḥaza 'be in love with one another, fall in love, become a lover, commit adultery, strive

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. mahida 'have labor pains' connected with the meaning 'shake, smite'. Ar. maḥada, Heb. māḥas; see maḥasa I, above. ETHIOPIC: Te. māḥas 'have labor pains' ṭā, Gur. (a)maṭā-čē; Te. māḥas 'labor pains', Amh. maṭ, Gur. māṭ; passed into Cushitic: 'birth pain'.
after someone's friendship, become intimate with, be flirtatious, (K) joke with one another (the Amharic translation tādarrā in H 224 does not mean 'agree' in this context, but 'be flirtatious')

phonetics: (maḥazātā ṣor 'porters', lit. 'young men for the load')

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. māḥaza 'one of the same age', Te. mazay 'best friend', Arg. munhinžed, Har. moḥaz māläq 'best man in a wedding', Amh. mize, maže; also in Cushitic: Bil. mada (with alternance d/z), Kham. maza. Qem. māğa, Or. minžē, minzē. The derivation of Amh. waza 'joke, mockery' from maḥaza suggested by Praetorius 1879:58 is unlikely.
tamāḥbara, passive; also 'be an associate' Denominative from māḥ(ə)bar, for which see ḥabara; also Te. 'amāḥbara 'go to a meeting'.

meaning: (Sw. in D 1396, under ḥadama), məḥdāmāt 'spoon for administering the Eucharist'
origin_explanation: perhaps from Ar. ḥadama 'serve', Chr.-Ar. ḥadama l-quddāsa 'celebrate Mass'.
amḥada, (L) 'amḥada 'suffer labor pains', (K) 'cause to suffer labor pains'

meaning: 'tower, fort, fortress, citadel, temple, (Lt) obelisk, statue; sign of Scorpion (zodiac)'
origin_explanation: tamāḥafada 'be built (tower)'; Denominative; SEMITIC: SAr. mḥfd 'tower' (with ḥ, and the Geez noun is perhaps māḥəfad rather than maḥəfad). ETHIOPIC: Tna. maḥəfad 'fort, fortress', 'strong wall and tower' (from Geez).

meaning: 'the veils and napkins with which an oblation is covered'
origin_explanation: (see Euringer, ZS 10[1935].135-6; Hammerschmidt 1967:128); from Ar. maḥfaza 'that in which a thing is kept'; also Tna. maḥfäd 'cloth placed over the paten before communion', Amh. maḥfäd (from Geez).
seems to be a misprint for massala.

meaning: 'hobble a cow by tying the hind legs together'
origin_explanation: Denominative from *maḥlak, from ḥlk, for which cp. Tna. ḥalākā 'hobble a cow by tying the hind legs together', Te. ḥallāka, Amh. allākā.

phonetics: māḥaməme (instead of māḥame of T), māḥaməme (T, reprint)

origin_explanation: see mawaḳa.

meaning: 'redouble, double, make twofold, duplicate, repeat, do a second time, multiply, increase'
origin_explanation: (e.g. 'amakʔaba šalasəta 'əda 'he trebled', 'increased threefold'); tamakʔaba, passive; denominative from makʔəbit 'double'; see kaʔaba.
denominative from makʔəbit; see kaʔaba, mkʔəb.

meaning: (T.Sw. in D 1394) 'mow, cut with a sickle'
origin_explanation: see also makala, kʔärläwa.
see also meʔekebe.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. makdi 'hilt of a sword', Amh. mäkde; also in Cushitic: Khamta makdi.
tamakkaḥa 'praise oneself, glorify oneself, boast, brag, puff up, be honored'
'magnificent'

meaning: 'boasting, glory, praise, glorifying, object of boasting, pride'
origin_explanation: (ʔalbäsa məkḥ [Lt] 'gala dress')
tamakkaḥo (Gr.101). part. of tamakkaḥo; 'vain person, braggart'

tamkaḥt 'glory, vainglory, glorification, boasting, insolence, impudence'; tamkaḥt (pl. of təmkaḥt) 'honors'; ETHIOPIC: Te. (ta)mäkkäḥa 'brag, rely on', Tna. (tä)mäkkäḥe, Amh. (tä)mäkka, Gur. (tä)mäkka 'rely on'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. mäkäläla 'mow, cut', Te. mäklay (pl. mäkäl) 'halm of durra, halm of corn' (Littmann-Höfner 131). See also makʔəläwa.

phonetics: maklit I (pl. makälay, makälayät, makäli, makälit)
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. kāla (kyl) 'measure' (v), Šn. mičōl 'measure' (n), from kyl > čyl (by palatalization). ETHIOPIC: Amh. mäklit 'talent (of silver)', from Geez; Te. käyyälä 'measure' is an Arabic loanword. For the weight of the maklit, see Pankhurst 1970, 1:55.
from Ar. miḥlāt 'fodder bag'; also in Te. mäklat 'fodder bag', Tna. mäk'lat, Bil.Sa. maklät (from Tigre).

meaning: 'box, chest, coffer, (T*) mat made of reed'
origin_explanation: Dillmann 1401 states that it is a transcription of Heb. mikmās, place-name.

meaning: (Gr.103) makkana 'be barren, be unfruitful, be sterile, be childless, be bereaved'
origin_explanation: makkana 'cause to be barren, bereave'; 'amkana, (Gr.103) 'amakkana, causative; also 'deprive of children'
makkān 'barren, unfruitful, sterile, childless (man or woman)'

meaning: makkäne (Gr.103), see mäknat; mäknat 'barrenness, sterility, bereavement, childlessness'
origin_explanation: mamkən, mamkani (K), part. of ʾamkana; ETHIOPIC: Te.Amh.Gur. mäkkän 'sterile', Tna. mäkan, Arg.Gaf. mähan; passed into Cushitic: Kham. mähan, Qem. mähan.
from Ar. maknun.
probably corruption of Ar. kānūn.
From Ar. miknasa.

meaning: 'pedestal, base, stand, (Gr.103) base for a jar, drinking gourd, dipper, storage bin'
origin_explanation: from Gk. mekhonōt, transcription of Heb. mäkonōt 'bases'.

meaning: 'make excuses, pretend, give a reason, use as a pretext, look for excuses'
origin_explanation: tamaknaya 'use as a pretext, look for a pretext, seek an occasion, look for means or for for a way, scheme'; tamakäna 'devise vicious actions against someone'; mäknayät 'pretext, (false) excuse, cause, reason, occasion, opportunity, means, wiles, subterfuge'; (za-mäknayät 'occasional'); ʾamaknayo (t) 'reason, argument'; ʾamaknäyi (Gr) 'one who makes excuses, cunning'; tamaknäyo (t) 'excuse, pretext, cause'; SEMITIC: Dillmann 857 reduces mkny to a root kny and compares it, among others, with Ar. kanā (kny) 'allude to, speak allusively of'. ETHIOPIC: Te. (ʾa)mäkänna 'use as a pretext', Tna. (ʾam)mäkanäyä, Amh. (ʾam)mäkanäna, Arg. (ʾam)-mäkanäna, Gur. (ʾam)mäkanäna; Tna. mäknayät 'pretext', Amh. mäknayät, mäkännat, Gur. mäknat, myat.

meaning: 'exhort, recommend, devise, form a plan, take counsel, determine, propose, make a decision, plot, conspire'
origin_explanation: ʾamkara 'advise, counsel, give a plan, ask for advice, devise a plan, persuade'; tamakara 'take counsel'; tamakära 'advise one another, take counsel together, consult, deliberate, devise a plan, conspire, plot'; ʾastamäkara (K*), caus. of tamakara; makāri, act. part. of makara; also 'prudent, counselor, advisor, plotter'; mäkur (K*), pass. part. of makara; mäkr 'plan, design, counsel, advice, deliberation, consultation, device, opinion, purpose, point of view, decision, understanding, judgment, conspiracy, intrigue, plot'; (bä-mäkr 'insidiously'); ʾamakäri, part. of ʾamkara; also 'applicant'; ʾamakäräyan, pl. of ʾamakäri (Gr.449) 'postulant monks'; mamkər (pl. mamkärän, mamäkärt) 'counselor, one who gives advice, advisor'; mamkäri, same; SEMITIC: Ar. makara 'deceive', makr 'craftiness, cunning', Heb. mäkera 'counsel' (Ludolf 87, 639, so also Ullendorff 193, Baumgartner 551), mäkar 'practice deceit' (D.W. Thomas, JTS 37[1936].388-9), Akk. mäkru 'counsel'. The connection between Ar.G. mkra 'beguile, plot' and Heb.Aram.Syr. mkr 'sell' suggested by Brockelmann 1928:385 (following Jensen) and Rabin in Ben-Hayyim 485 is doubtful. Concerning these roots Noeldeke 1910:76, n. 4 states, "Dies mir [Heb. Syr.] mag in letzter Instanz mit مكر 'versuchen, raten' identisch sein, aber die Bedeutungsübergänge wären schwer festzustellen." ETHIOPIC: Te. mäkra 'advise', Tna. mäkära, Amh. mäkära, Arg. mäkära, Gur. mäkära. The root passed into Cushitic: Be. mekir 'advise', Kham. mäkər, Sa. makar.

meaning: 'tempt, try, test, put to the test, examine, choose by testing'
origin_explanation: umakkara, causative; also 'test, try, tempt, examine, prove, explore'; tamakkara, pass. of makkara; mäkkər 'tested, tried, experienced'; makära 'trial, testing, examination, temptation, tribulation, distress, ordeal'; makäräwi (Lt) 'tested, tried, stricken with misfortune'; ʾamakkara (Lt), part. of ʾamakkara; tamakkäro (Lt) 'temptation'; tamkäri (Lt) 'temptation, trial'; ETHIOPIC: Tna. mäkkärä 'attempt, try, test', Amh. mokkärä, Arg. mokkärä; passed into Cushitic: Or. mokkora. Note that Dillmann 198-9 and Noeldeke 1910:76, n. 4 combine mkr I and mkr II. In view of Amh.Arg. mokkärä, mokkära, it is possible that the root for 'try, test' is m*kr.

meaning: name of the sixth Coptic month (it corresponds to G. yakkä-til)
origin_explanation: from Coptic mekhir; see also mäšir, below, and ʾamšər, above.

(za-mak*räb 'second Sunday of Lent'); SEMITIC: Sar. mkrb 'shrine, temple, synagogue' (?), from krb I (according to SabDic 78), and perhaps also krb-t 'blessing' even though SabDic 79 considers it as coming from krb II; cp. also Akk. karābu 'pray'. On a bibliography of G. mäk*räb and Sar. mkrb, see Landberg 1909:642ff. Buhl 117 (followed by Baumgartner 152) connects G. mäk*räb with brk (pi'el) 'bless'. Fraenkel 274 derives Ar. mihrāb 'niche in a mosque, synagogue' from G. mäk*räb (from which he also derives, with reservations, Heb. karūb 'cherub'), against Praetorius, ZDMG 61 (1907).621-2, Landberg 1909:641ff., Noeldeke 1910:52, n. 3, and Rhodokanakis, Wörter und Sachen 3 (1911).125, n. 6. ETHIOPIC: Tna. mäk*räb 'synagogue', Amh. mäk*räb (from Geez).
is probably an interpretation of the French 'microbe'.
from krk < krkr; see karkara, above. For krk < krkr, cp. also Gur. karäkä 'grind coarsely'.
see karaya I.

meaning: 'referring to the flame, epithet of Christ, (T) a cry of woe'
origin_explanation: comes from Gk. (mnēsthetēi) mou kyrie ('remember me') oh Lord!'; see also muvagäyä, mäddäšä.
the meaning 'millipede' is confirmed by KG makraz = Amh. yä-ʾabbätät tal 'worm that is found in dung'.

meaning: has a variety of meanings which seem to belong to different roots: 1) 'accuse, boast, bet'; 2) 'discharge pus'; 3) 'guard, protect one another'; 4) 'take by force'. Note that makk*aya has the same meanings.

origin_explanation: perhaps in connection with mak*si 'of the same name'.
perhaps a misprint for mak*asiär ʾəhə 'broom', for which see k*astara.
tamak*äsaya (Gr), same; ʾamak*saya (K*), causative; denominative from mak*si, mak*say 'namesake' (see k*asaya 'be nicknamed'); cp. also Amh. (tä)mokäššä 'be nicknamed'.
see mäʾək*at, above.
is perhaps a misprint for tamak*asa ʾəhə 'accuse one another', for which see mak*asa, above.

meaning: has the same meanings as mak*asa, above.
origin_explanation: for the meanings 'watch, guard' Dillmann 202 compares Ar. maqā (mqy) 'watch, guard'.
from Gk. malakos 'soft, unmanly'
-hī could be a suffix considering the number of nouns ending in a non-etymological -hī (see Tayyā 92) in view of melānī. below, the form milāhī could be a misprint

(denominative of malḥ); (note that G. መለሐ malḥa or mallaha is rendered by T 37 and TS 9 'do, work' (also malḥ 'work'), possibly 'do good work', salt being the symbol of good deeds. It could also be መለሐ malḥa is for መለአ malʾa below, and the general meaning 'do, work' refers to all the activities expressed by malḥa); tamallaha, passive of mallaha; also 'be salty'; malḥ (TS 9), malḥā (D), malḥ (Gr.K*) 'salt, savor, judgment, common sense, intellect, insight, discernment, knowledge'; (bäḥra malḥ [Lt] 'Dead Sea'); mallāḥi (Gr.74), act. part. of mallaha; malluḥ (Gr.74), pass. part. of mallaha; malḥ (K*) 'seasoned with salt, salty, (T) which is tasty'; mallāḥe (Gr.74) 'seasoning with salt, common sense'; malḥat (Gr.74), same; SEMITIC: Ar. milḥ 'salt', Soq. milḥo, Heb. melaḥ, Md. miḥla, Syr. melḥā, Md. miḥla, Ph. mlḥ 'salt' Akk. milḥu 'potassium' (von Soden 653), malḥuṭu 'ein salziges Gras' (von Soden 596), Gordon 443 considers Ug. mlḥ 'good' (also Ar. malīḥ 'pleasant'). Ullendorf, JSS 7 (1962), 345 suggests Ug. mlḥ in ḥrb mlḥt 'salted knife' (see also Caquot 154; Fronzaroli 1972, vii 621 translates it 'a good knife'). ETHIOPIC: Te. mälḥa 'to salt', Amh. mälḥ 'salt' (from G. malḥa) (Som. milḥ 'salt', Sa. milḥ, Af. mulḥu) is borrowed from Arabic.

meaning: tear out, pluck out, pull out, eradicate, eliminate, take off, weed, draw (a sword), drag forth, (T) throw away
origin_explanation: note that (T.Y) 'scintillate' seems to be a transferred meaning; ʾamlaḥa (K*), causative;

meaning: passive of malḥa; also 'fall out, fall off, be thrown, uproot oneself'
origin_explanation: tamallāḥa (K*) 'draw (swords) on one another'; ʾastamallāḥa 'cause to draw (a sword)'; ʾastamallāḥa (K*), caus. of tamallāḥa; malḥāḥi, act. part. of malḥa; malḥuḥ, (T) malḥ, pass. part. of malḥa; malḥat, verbal noun of malḥa; SEMITIC: Ar. malaḥa 'pull out', Hars. melōḥ 'pull down', Heb. mlḥ (nif-al) 'be torn up, be dispersed in fragments', Akk. malāḥu 'remove, tear apart' (considered by von Soden 593 related to Aram. malaq 'pluck'). ETHIOPIC: Te. mälḥa 'draw a sword' (that is, 'pull from the sheath'), also malḥay (for malḥay) gäbʾa 'be stripped' (curse), Tna. mälḥe 'pull out', mälämmälä, Amh. mälämmälä 'cut' (from mlḥ > ml, reduplicated into mlml), Gur. mälä; passed into Cushitic: Sa. mulūḥ 'pulled out'. See also malḥota, below.
denominative from malḥat; see malḥa.

meaning: is the same root as mlk, below, but for the sake of convenience the two entries are separated; malaka 'own (property), possess, have dominion, dominate, become master, take possession of, occupy, rule, reign, have authority'
origin_explanation: malaka (M) 'be established as owner, be subjected (to)'; ʾamlaka, caus. of malaka; tamalka, pass. of malaka; also 'be subjected to, be made (constituted) master'; malāki, act. part. of malaka; also 'master, ruler, heir'; mālūk 'dominated, subjected, possessed'; malkot 'lordship, kingdom, domination'; malkat 'lordship, kingdom, domination, empire'; malkat 'property, possession, ownership'; malkannā 'ownership, possession, rule, dominion, power, domination, lordship, rulership, authority, sovereignty'; ʾamlākot 'domination'; tamlāki 'principality, possession, authority, dominion';

SAr. mlk 'become king', Sh. śmlak 'take possession', Heb. mālāk 'reign, rule', Aram.Syr. ma-lak, Md.Ug.Ph. mlk; Akk. malāku 'rule' is a West Semitic loanword. For 'king', cp. Ar. malik, SAr. mlk, Heb. melek, Aram-Syr. malkā, Md. malka, Akk. malku.
Tna. mälakä 'possess, dominate, rule'.

meaning: 'worship God, worship a deity, wor-ship idols' (denominative)
'Godhead, divinity, deity, divine na-ture, divine rule'
origin_explanation: (for its Aramaic origin, see Noeldeke 1910:33)

meaning: 'one who worships, worshiper, one who fears God, pious, religious'

origin_explanation: Te. amlāka, Amh. amlākā; denominative from amlak 'God', for which cp. Te. amlak, Amh. amlak, Amh.Gur. amlākannāt 'deity'.
from Gk. malakhē 'mallow' through Ar. malūhiya; also in Amh. muluki(y)e, Syr. mülūkyā.
(Velat 99ff)
Amh.Gur. (tä)mäläkätä. Praetorius 1879:139 de-rives it from *mälkät, from G. läk'a 'design, mark'. See also malakat, below.
(see Powne 36-9)

Tna. mäläkāt; passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af. malakat, Or. malakata. Coulbeaux-Schreiber 127 translate Tna. mäläkāt 'instrument de signal, trompette', and seem to derive it from mlkt 'fix one's attention, point at', for which see malkata, above.
from Chr.-Ar. malaki, so called because they adopted the dogmatic definition of the court (from Syr. malkā 'king, emperor').
an interpretation of the proper name Malachias, Heb. mal'āki (for which see Mal. 1:1).

(note that M 48 renders it 'suckle, anoint, plane', a possible translation of Amh. lagā that renders G. malala; but on the basis of the Amharic translations laṭā, arrāgā, and others, given by KWK for G. malala, the most ap-propriate rendering is 'plane (a board, smooth with a plane').
perhaps Heb. mll 'circumcise', secondary form of mwl.
mälämmālā 'hew, trim or prune (a tree)', Tna. mäläll bälä 'extract', mälmālā 'prune a tree'.
Tna. mälāl bälä 'be tall', Amh. mälāl bälä 'be straight and tall'.
from Gk. melīne 'millet'; see also milāhi, above.
probably from Gk. melandetos 'bound or mounted with black' (W. Müller).
from Gk. melanthion 'black cumin'; also in Amh. mäläns 'oilseed plant' (from Geez).
with inserted n in relation to melātrā, below.
from Gk. melopepōn 'melon'; also in PbH. möläpa-pōn 'watermelon', Syr. malpōnā, möläpöpōnā.

meaning: 'cut, sever, pluck, break off, snap off'

origin_explanation: Heb. mäläq 'nip off', Aram.Syr. malaq 'peel, pluck off'.
Tna. mälqäqa 'snatch, tear away', mäläqä 'a 'get loose, get disen-gaged', Amh. mäläqqägä 'husk corn', möläqqäqä 'tear out of the hand'.

phonetics: miläq, miläqäqä (T.M.) miläq, miläqäq (K), pl. miläqāt (D)
meaning: 'small basket, fruit basket, basket serving as a meas-ure'
origin_explanation: from Gk. malakia, nom.acc. pl. of malakion 'small basket made of palm leaves'.

meaning: 'gleam, shine, glitter, sparkle, be polished, (Y) polish, smooth, wipe clean'

origin_explanation: Har. (a)mäläsa 'have a delicate and smooth appearance'.

meaning: 'come and go, go to and fro'

origin_explanation: from Amh. mälläsä 'return, bring back'. The root also occurs in Tna. mälläsä 'give back, answer'.

meaning: 'precious stone, fiery sword, (Lt) sword and arrow of divinity, (T) Holy Spirit' (magic word)
origin_explanation: for the origin of melos (also melyos, and others) as coming from the name of Solomon being inverted, see Worrell II:98, n. 5, Euringer, ZS 9 (1933-4): 84, and Hammerschmidt 1961:162, as against Streleyn 1955:419.
corrupt from aslāmāwi 'Muslim' (Praetorius, ZD-MG 64[1910],621-2); also considered a folk etymolo-gy from mälläsä 'turn apostate'.

meaning: 'be tasty, be delicious, be savory, please, content (tr)'

origin_explanation: note that Tayyā 184 has malisu qasu, both words meaning 'monkey'. He quotes them together be-cause of their ending -şu.

meaning: 'purple, scarlet, fine linen, pure linen, (T.M) white silk'
origin_explanation: from Aram.Syr. melātā ('ämilātā) 'fine wool, cloak of fine wool', Syr. milātā 'kind of carpet'. For its connection with G. hamelat, see Noeldeke 1910:39. The root is possibly connected with Ar. milāya(t), mulāya(t), malā'a(t) 'kind of mantle', for which see Dozy 1845:401-11.

meaning: 'confuse, move the hand', 'cause to slap' (denominative from mal-tähä)
'hold the cheeks between the hands, lay the hands upon the cheeks, lay the'
could there be a connection with maleto, referring to the turban, the head covering of priests?

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. moltān 'litany' (from Geez), Tna. amaltānā 'chant a litany'; to be connected with liton, above.

meaning: wrongly copied from the line below, as is evident from KG 2, 3 where G. malāši is rendered by Amh. malāč 'shaver'.

origin_explanation: Guidi 1906:919 considers mālāwə a misprint for mālāwə galaw. In this case mālāwə will also have to be considered a misprint of mālāwə galawa.

phonetics: malaya I (T.Y.K.M) 'separate, divide, dis-tinguish, guess, pull away, tear out, liquefy'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. mallāyya, mallāyyo 'distinguishing feature, attribute', from lāyyā 'separate, distinguish', for which see lelaya; perhaps also Tna. mālāyyo 'ruban d'étoffe dont on se ceint le front, pour arrêter l'effusion du beurre fondu qui découle des tresses de la coiffure' (for which see Coulbeaux-Schreiber 128). This would also imply the meaning of 'separate'.

meaning: is strange in relation to malaya I.

also with an additional interrogative -nu in another element of the sentence; e.g. amsa-māy-nu wamimma amsab 'from heaven or men?'; mimma...wamimma 'either...or, whether...or' composed of the interrogative -mi and the enclitic -mma, above. Dillmann 1907:118 considers -mimma an interrogative element.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. mami 'or what?'.

phonetics: mam'ala (MA) = Amh. wāggālā which MA 51 translates 'préparer une peau tanner', while the existing dictionaries have meaning 'drag, pull'. Amharic wāggālā to stand for wānāggālā 'charge with a crime, and mam'ala would then have the same meaning as mam'ela, mam'ala, below.
mamma'a 'be timid, be confounded, be terrified, be frightened, be panicked, become alarmed, be dismayed, draw back (out of fear), be mad'
meaning: amamma'a, a causative; also 'confound, to frighten'; mammā'i (Gr.81) 'fearful'; mammu'a 'fearful, frightened, terrified, panicky'; mammā'e, (Gr.) mammā 'fear, terror, disarray, anxiety, panic'; (ambala mammā'e 'intrepidly'); mammā'a 'anxiety, terror, consternation, cowardice, slothfulness'.
origin_explanation: from mm, and could be connected with ma'a-ma'a 'be angry, be irritated'. See also ma'ama, mammata.

phonetics: mam'ela (T.M), mam'ala (TS) 'be treacherous, be unfaithful, perjure oneself'
meaning: denominative from mam'ali, for which see m'ly. See also mam'ala, above.

phonetics: mammaṭa (Sw. in D 1393, T.Y) 'be seized with panic, flee, (Y) be afflicted, (K*) diminish, be deficient'
meaning: mammāṭe 'defect, (Lt) bruises, (T.M) panic, flight, affliction, (Sw. in D 161) sorrow, (K*) deficiency'; see also mamma'a.

phonetics: mamtaq, mamtaqi, mamtaqit, mamṭāq 'leather sack or bag, (T.M) belt, (T.M) sling or part of a sling'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: if 'sling' is the original meaning, cp. Amh. (a)māṭṭāqā 'propel'.

meaning: from mymy, and goes back to māy 'water'. Note that Amh. m'am'a 'dissolve' goes back to moḥwa, above, rather than to memaya.

phonetics: memaya II (Sw. in D 1393, T.M) 'separate rate, distinguish'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. māmāyā 'winnow', that is, 'separate grain from the chaff'.
From Gk. manaa, transcription of Heb. minhā. See also maḥana II, above.
From Heb. mān through Gk. manna; also Te.Tna. Amh. mānna.

meaning: mandā`-tā (K 627, under nd`) 'disease of the chest and shoulder which causes swelling';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. mändə`at, mändə`at-i 'disease that attacks the spine', Te. mändə`at 'sciatica'. KWK 627 derives G. mandā` from nad`a 'drive' > *nd` 'drive'; disease derived from nd` 'drive', cp. also Amh. näddädä 'drive, have severe diarrhea'.

meaning: , amandaba 'torment, inflict pain, bring tribulation, afflict, cause trouble, reduce to distress, bring into difficulty, mortify';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. nädbä 'be angry, be sorry'. Tna. mändäbä 'torment', Amh. mändäddäbä 'afflict, inflict suffering'.

meaning: , passive; also 'suffer tribulation, be anguished, be in distress, be in danger, be in jeopardy';
origin_explanation: perhaps a denominative from mändäb, from ndb: Ar. nadaba 'mortify', nadab 'scar' (so also Rabin, JJS 6[1955].130; he also compares Heb. nādāb, originally 'the man obliged to fight in war'). It is tempting to connect it with Ar. mandab, in the name of the strait bābu-l-mandab which means 'gate of distress or difficulty', but W. Müller suggests that mandab is a place-name that also occurs in SAr. (late Sabaean) mdbn.

meaning: , pass. part. of amandaba; mändäbä 'tribulation, distress, affliction, anguish, calamity, adversity, torment, hardship, difficulty, misery';
origin_explanation: perhaps a denominative from mändäb, from ndb: Ar. nadaba 'mortify', nadab 'scar' (so also Rabin, JJS 6[1955].130; he also compares Heb. nādāb, originally 'the man obliged to fight in war'). It is tempting to connect it with Ar. mandab, in the name of the strait bābu-l-mandab which means 'gate of distress or difficulty', but W. Müller suggests that mandab is a place-name that also occurs in SAr. (late Sabaean) mdbn.

meaning: (T) = Amh. yätäggä bet, perhaps 'a household that never prospers', lit. 'a house that never goes beyond the calf stage'.

(pl. manādəl) 'sacerdotal vestment (Hammerschmidt 1970:155), female headdress, handkerchief, (Gr.99) garment'; mandala (K*) 'wash, clean (the body, the body)', that is to say, 'use the mandil to clean or dry oneself' (denominative);
origin_explanation: from Latin mantile through Gk. μανδῆλι, μανδίλι, or through Ar. mandīl (for which see Graf 108, Dozy 1845:414-8, and the detailed study by F. Rosenthal, in Four essays on art and life in Islam [1971]63-99); also Te.Tna.Amh. mandil, mändilā.

meaning: (T) 'torches made of twigs which the young people light for the festivity of the Cross or of St. John';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. mändältō, mändärto 'cudgel' (even though the meanings do not correspond). See also mandarto.
from Ar. bunduq, bundūq 'rifle' (with alternation of labials); also Te. manduq. Amh. bunduq, menduq.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. mänduqe 'garment that is girded about the waist'.
to be identified with mandalto, above, with alternation of liquids l:r.
from Gk. mandyē, mandyā 'woolen cloak'; Chr.-Ar. mantiyā 'bishop's cloak'. See also mäday.
probably misprint for mäsfar , below.
from Amh. manfiya (for which see Isenberg 34).

meaning: 'toward, to, by (place), the side of, by the side of, with regard to', with prepositions: bamangala 'by the side of, by toward, to'; lamangala 'toward, to, with regard to'; əmangala mangala, əmmangala 'toward, on the side of, from the side of, through, because of, due to, concerning'; haba mangala 'toward';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: The connection with Ar. nağada 'become manifest' suggested by Dillmann 685 (followed by Barth 1893:45 who connects it with Heb. neğed 'in sight of, before') is unlikely. Likewise unlikely is the suggestion made by Praetorius, ZDMG 57 (1903). 273 who identifies it with SAr. mnql 'path cut on the mountainside', and with G. mangad 'road'. Could it be connected with Ar. nağl 'broad road'?
denominative from *mangal, for which see nagala; also Amh. mänäggälä.

meaning: (pl. manägənt) 'engine of war, machine, device, scheme, ruse, trick, machination, instrument of torture, (T) fishhook, (K*) snare';
origin_explanation: from Gk. manganon 'means for bewitching, trick, engine of war, hunting net'; also in Aram.-Syr. mangānōn 'engine of war', Ar. mangānūn 'water engine, waterwheel'.

meaning: 'use a ruse, use a trick, devise a scheme, be in a difficult situation, be in want' (denominative);
origin_explanation: from Gk. manganon 'means for bewitching, trick, engine of war, hunting net'; also in Aram.-Syr. mangānōn 'engine of war', Ar. mangānūn 'water engine, waterwheel'.
from Gk. manganon 'means for bewitching, trick, engine of war, hunting net'; also in Aram.-Syr. mangānōn 'engine of war', Ar. mangānūn 'water engine, waterwheel'.
from Gk. manganon 'means for bewitching, trick, engine of war, hunting net'; also in Aram.-Syr. mangānōn 'engine of war', Ar. mangānūn 'water engine, waterwheel'.
from Ar. mağğan 'gratuitous' < Syr. mağğān (Schwally, ZDMG 53[1899].197). For this root in Semitic, see Koehler 493, under mgn I, Baumgartner 517, under mgn 1.

meaning: , manganiqā, mangalaq 'catapult, engine, arms, rifle, cannon';
origin_explanation: from Gk. manganikon, pl. manganika 'catapult, cannon' through Ar. mangānīq; also Syr. mangānīqā 'engine for hurling stones', Aram. mangānīqōn, Amh. māngānīq 'catapult, rifle'. On Ar. mangānīq see Ayalon 27-30.
see magnitos.

meaning: (T.D 1401) 'part of a house that is screened or partitioned off, something cut off'.
origin_explanation: transcription of Heb. munnāḥ 'that is left'.
from mənḥar, from nḥr 'flow'. See also mənḥal, and nähara.
for mənḥar, from nḥr 'flow'. See also mənḥal.

from Amh. manka 'spoon'; in the other Ethiopian languages: Te.Arg. mānka, Tna.Gaf. mānka, Manka, Gur. manka; passed into Cushitic: Bil. mānka, Kham. miyakā, mikā, Or. mānkā. Praetorius 1879:70 derives it from nky 'touch' (Amh. näkkä). Coulbeaux-Schreiber 481 (under näkäyā) derive Tna. manka from näkäyā 'take a bit, take off'.
see nakaba.
see also mag°napa, above.

meaning: (MA) monkosā 'be-come a monk, live a monastic life' (denomina-tive).
origin_explanation: amank°asa, causative; manakos (pl. manakost, manakosän, manakosāt) 'monk'; (manakos bäḥtäwi, manakos gadamāwi 'hermit, anchorite'); manakosäwi, manakosäy 'monastic'; manakosäwit, manakosäyit 'nun'; mənk°äsənna 'monastic life, ascetic life, monk-hood, monasticism, monastic ordination'; (məšafa mənk°äsənna 'Monastic Ritual'); mənk°äsənnawi 'referring to monastic life'; amank°äsi (Gr.98) 'who urges to monastic life'. From Gk. monakhos 'monk'; also in Tna. mänäkäsä 'become a monk', Amh. mänäkäsä 'become a monk', Amh. mäläkäsä 'become poor'. Te. mänkäsa 'become poor' is not to be derived from Ar. manḥūs 'unlucky, unprosperous', as suggested by Littmann-Höfner 741, since it is Arabic ḥ that becomes k in a loanword and not h. On monks and monasteries, see Cerulli, in OCA 153 (1958).259-60, Hyatt 65ff, Hammerschmidt 1967:121ff, Heyer 1945ff, 193ff.

meaning: despise, disdain, reject, renounce, repel, cast aside, hold in con-tempt, belittle'.
origin_explanation: amannana, causative; also 'render contemptible'; tamannana, passive of mannana; mannäni, act. part. of mannana; (mannäne 'alam 'monk'); mannən 'rejected, despised, re-nounced, repudiated, despicable, abominable, contemptible, repulsed, vile, worth-less, useless'; mənänne, (Lt) mənänän 'rejection, disdain, vile-ness, repudiation, renunciation, worthlessness, wickedness'; mannənot (Lt) 'rejection'. SEMITIC: Dillmann 189 connects it with mnn 'refuse': Heb. me'en, Syr. me(e)n 'be reluctant, be unwill-ing'; and mn' 'deny, refuse': Ar. man'a, na'a. The comparison that Baumgartner 570 sug-gests, with reservations, with Heb. mnn (mnn) 'reproach someone for (a gift)' is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Te. mänänna 'decline', Tna. mänänwä 'be disgusted, reject'. Reinsch 1887:358 also compares Tna. wänänä, same. The root is probably passed into Cushitic: Bil. minin y, Qem. mänän, Sa. binin ya.

meaning: (K*) 'be insignificant, incomplete, be deficient in, lack, dwindle'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. mänänna 'become lean or emaci-ated', manamən 'without value', Amh. mänämänä 'become lean, thin, emaciated', mänänna 'be inferior, be of poor quality'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. mən(n)an. See also Baumgartner, under männe, Kaufman 69, and Dillmann 190. For the weight of G. mənänän, see Pankhurst 1970, 1:54. See on the root Eilers 616.
from Gk. monokerōtos, gen.sg. of monokerōs 'uni-corn' (Hommel 1877:68).
from Aram.Syr. mənārātā; also Ar. manārā(t). See Fraenkel 270-1, and marana, below.

meaning: (T) = Amh. aškäla 'a creep-er that is used for making snares for birds or traps for animals'.
origin_explanation: for manasäg°t and to be identified with manasäg°, pl. manasäg° 'snare' (see nasaga, below).

meaning: (Gr.96,T.Y) 'be in dan-ger, be about to perish, be led to temptation, wander about aimlessly' (denominative from mansut).
origin_explanation: amansawa 'lead to temptation, expose to tribulation or peril, bring about a disaster or calami-ty, destroy'. tamannasawa 'be tried, be tested, be tempted, be led to temptation, run the risk of, be given to passion'; tamannasäwa (K*) 'test one another'; mansut (pl. manasəw) 'temptation, test, trial, peril, affliction, distress, calamity, offense, cause of stumbling, divine punishment, wrath, indignation, retribution'; mənsäw (fem. mənsut) 'tempted, tried, subject to temptation or peril or affliction, one who se-duces'; mənsäwe 'temptation, peril, affliction, (Gr.431) wrath'; amansäwi (Gr.97), part. of 'amansawa; goes back to *nasawa, below; ETHIOPIC: Amh. mansut 'tribulation', mänässätä 'trouble, disturb, upset' (denominative).
ETHIOPIC: perhaps to be derived from mnsq: Amh. mänätäqä 'snatch away, tear off'.

meaning: (L) 'cut down trees, deforest; mansar (Lt) 'clearing the trees (n), deforesting (n)'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. mänätärä 'cut down trees'.

meaning: monastery, cloister'; ('abba manet, liqa manet 'abbot of a monas-tery'); ('əmma manet 'abbess of a monastery').
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ludolf 83, 639 derives it from mny 'de-sire, wish'. Dillmann 194 seems to derive it from a root mny 'separate, seclude' represented in min, mən 'from'. Noeldeke, WZKM 20 (1906). 222 considers it a borrowing from Gk. mone 'monastery'. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. manet 'monastery' (from Geez).

meaning: 'what?', məntə-nu 'what? why?', məntəni, məntəhi 'any, anything, whatever, something', məntənumma 'what then?', 'imənt, 'iməntəni, 'iməntəhi 'nothing, nothing whatever, not any', wə'iməntəni wə'iməntəhi 'nothing whatsoever', wə'ikama məntəni 'as nothing'; kama wə'iməntəni ('əni) 'in a moment of time, in a flash'; with prepositions ləmənt, ləməntənu 'why?', bə'əmənt, bə'əməntəna 'what for what reason?', bə'əmənt 'of which?', bəmənt, bəməntənu 'in what way?', bəməntəni 'in any way', zəmənt 'of which?', 'əskə mənt, 'əskə məntənu 'how long?', kama məntəni 'like nothing, like something of little value'; with suffixes: məntəka 'əllu 'what are these to thee?'. note məntə konka 'what is troubling thee?, what happened to thee?'. for other details, see Dillmann 1907:334-5.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Akk. minnu 'what?'. For the interrogative particle in Semitic, see Brockelmann 1908:326, Barth 1913:142-3. ETHIOPIC: Tna. mənow, mənu 'which?', Te. mə, mi, Amh.Arg.Gaf.Gur. mən 'what?', Har. min. For the final -t, see Tna. məntay. Eitan, AJSL 44 (1937-8): 255 analyzes G. mənt as mə-əntə or ma-əntə, lit. 'what (is it) that?'. Littmann 1909:64:359 analyzes it as mi-nt. See also Reinisch 1909:61, Rundgren 1955:199, 249.

meaning: 'be twinborn, bear twins' (denominative); 'əməntəwa 'bear twins, (Gr.97) perform two equal acts'; ('əməntəwa ləbbəunəhu 'he deflected, he deviated, he was undecided'); təməntəwa 'be twinborn'; məntəw (pl. məntəw) 'twins, both'; məntəwi 'twin'; məntəw (fem. məntut) 'twinborn, doubled'; məntəwə 'doubling, duality, condition of being one of twins'; məntəwənnä (K*), same;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: The connection with Heb. tə'ōm-im 'twins', Ar. tu'ām suggested by Dillmann 189 (so also Armbruster 183, and Leslau 1958:55) is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh.Arg.Gaf. məntäw 'twin', Te. mäntäy, Gur. mältä, Har. mälta; denominative: Tna. mäntäwä 'bear twins', Te. mäntä 'multiply rapidly', Amh. mänätä 'be forked, be twins'; perhaps from Cushitic: Bil. mättä 'twin', Qem. mätä, Qab.Al. mälta-ta, Kafa mätä.
from mtn (see mətna) with reduplication of the last two radicals and insertion of n. For more examples of reduplication, see Dillmann 1907:232.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. məntitə 'turtledove'.

meaning: 'wish, desire, be eager for'; 'əmənnaya (Lt), causative; təməny, fem. mənit (Gr.99) 'who wishes ardently, wished for, desired, desirous'; təmənni 'wish, desire, lust'; təmənniyä (Gr.99) 'who wishes, who desires'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (tā)mənna (mny) 'wish, desire', Soq. motene; perhaps Akk. menū, manū 'love' (von Soden 645). Jensen 1887:427-8 connects it with mny 'count' (Heb.Aram.Syr., also Ug. Akk), for which he gives the original meaning 'think > wish'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tä)mänäyä 'wish, desire', Te. (tä)männa, Amh. (tä)männa, Arg. (əm)männa, Gaf. (tä)männa, Gur. (tä)männa, Har. (tä)männa passed into Cushitic: Bil. menä-.
gives the impression of being a compound; see also mäyəbäli. An unpublished Säwäsəw has also mäyəbäli (Getatchew).

meaning: 'live comfortably, be pampered, be spoiled, be delicate, be soft'; mänzəh 'spoiled, pampered, delicate' (Gr.99 = Amh. dələddəl 'honnête homme constant dans son propos'; while it is true that Amh. dələddəl also means 'sincere, loyal', the meaning of dələddəl is also 'one who is well-off and comfortable', and this is the meaning of dələddəl in this context);
origin_explanation: the root is augmented by n in relation to mzh (see mazha, below). See also ləmənzəh.
from Ar. manāzil, for which see Landberg 1942:2763.

meaning: 'commit adultery, be licentious'; manzər, (K) manzär 'bastard, dissolute, (KG) 'who squanders'; manzərän (T) 'dissolute, loose, profligate' (is probably a plural of manzər);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: possibly from the root mdr, mzr (Ar.Heb. Syr) 'be rotten', with the prefix m- represented in Hebrew, Aramaic as mamzer 'bastard' becoming mnzr in Ethiopic (so Dillmann 191, Praetorius 1879: 59, Noeldeke 1910:46). It could also be interpreted as mdr, mzr with a prefixed m- (as mamzer in Heb. Aram) and with an infixed n as in Ethiopic mnzr. There is also the possibility of connecting the Ethiopic root with mnzr (Te.Tna.Amh) 'waste money, be spendthrift', becoming 'be dissolute, be debauched' (in G. Tna.Amh). In this case G. manzər 'bastard, dissolute' would not be identified with Semitic (Heb. Aram) mamzer 'bastard'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (a)mänzärä 'fornicate, commit adultery', Amh. (a)mänzärä.

meaning: 'wander about restlessly or erratically (e.g. one who is upset or disturbed), (K*) be importunate, pester with demands'; 'əmənzaza 'insult, revile, reproach, afflict, provoke, oppress, persecute, pursue with words, (T) be stubborn, persist'; təmənzaza (Gr.99), passive of 'əmənzaza; manzuz, pass. part. of 'əmənzaza; manzäze (Gr.99), verbal noun of 'əmənzaza; manzəzo (T) 'wandering about aimlessly'; 'əmənzäzi (Gr.99), part. of 'əmənzaza;
origin_explanation: perhaps a denominative from *mnzz, from a root nzz and to be compared with nznz (reduplicated root nzz), for which see naznaza 'vex, torment' below.

probably a misprint for maq'ad (see qalada I), since on the preceding line (T 175) there is maq'ad = Amh. san 'small basin'. Note, however, that Sw. in D 1394 also has maqd = Amh. san, and Dillmann, ib. suggests, with reservations, correcting it into maqədḥi 'small basin' (see qədḥa).

meaning: (L.D) 'basin used for washing hands when entering the church';
origin_explanation: from qdm 'be in front', the basin being placed at the front of the entrance to church.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps related to Tna. maqätä 'disdain, despise'.

meaning: 'fluff up cotton for carding'; məntäf 'fluffed cotton ready for carding';
origin_explanation: denominative from məntäf, from ntf; see natafa.
ETHIOPIC: Te. mäntälle 'hare', Tna. mäntilä, mäntäle, Amh. mäntäle. Brockelmann 1950:24 considers it a Cushitic loanword and compares it with Bil.

ETHiOPiC: Tna. māqālā 'divide'. In fact, since it comes from Yāqōb Gābrā Iyyäsus, a Tigrinya speak- er, it is possible that G. maqala is taken from Tigrinya. SEMiTiC: M. Bittner, WZKM 30 (1917-8).423-4 compares it with Soq. meqədaḥ < *məqadaḥ < *məqadaṭ 'pot'. However, the identification of this root with Amh. mənčāt 'pot' suggested by Bittner, ib. is unlikely. ETHiOPiC: Har. māqāṭu 'clay pot'.

phonetics: maqādəw መቃድው, (T) maqāśəw መቃሽው; see maqduṭ.
maqfart መቅፈርት, in təbəlayā zamaqfart ren- ders Heb. עפוד 'a priest's garment (Ex. 28:4), (T) kind of shawl-like garment made of a single layer of cloth' (Amh. nāṭalā).
meaning: in təbəlayā zamaqfart ren- ders Heb. עפוד 'a priest's garment (Ex. 28:4), (T) kind of shawl-like garment made of a single layer of cloth' (Amh. nāṭalā).

phonetics: moqaḥa ሞቀሐ 'put in chains, bind with fetters, take prisoner, cast into prison';
meaning: put in chains, bind with fetters, take prisoner, cast into prison';
origin_explanation: SEMiTiC: Hommel 108 identifies it with Ar. waqaḥa 'hardhoofed' from waqaḥa 'be hard (hoof) > hard- hearted, merciless', moqaḥ coming from mwaqaḥ. Prae- torius, ZDMG 57 (1903). 274 derives moqaḥ from *malqaḥ (however, there are no examples in Geez for -al becoming o), from lqh 'take, seize > take a prisoner by putting him in chains'. Neither of these suggestions is convincing. ETHiOPiC: Te. moqaḥa 'fetter', Tna. moqeḥe, māqəḥe, Amh. əgrā muq 'leg-irons'; also in Cushitic: Bil.Sa. mōqoḥ 'chain'.
ETHiOPiC: Tna. maq 'coarse woolen cloth', Amh. maqqaqā 'wear a coarse woolen cloth, scratch the face with a coarse cloth (mourner)'.

phonetics: maqlaqa I መቅለቃ (T.K*D 1394) 'burn, sting (e.g. medicine), burn the throat, cauterize';
meaning: burn, sting (e.g. medicine), burn the throat, cauterize';
origin_explanation: ETHiOPiC: Tna. māqəmāq bālā 'burn (pepper)'.

phonetics: maqlaqa III መቅለቃ (K*) 'melt (intr), dissolve (intr), be spoiled';
origin_explanation: SEMiTiC: Heb. mqq (nif-al) 'dissolve', Aram. (it)- maqmaq 'melt away'.
