ETHiOPiC: Te. māqəmaqo 'a bitter herb used to clari- fy butter', Tna. māq 'māqəqo, Amh. māqəmāqo, māqqa (maqq) 'melt', Amh. māqqaqā, māqqaqā 'be- come rotten, become spoiled'. The root is related to mkk: Tna. mākäkā 'melt'.

phonetics: maqqara መቅቀረ (Y) 'be properly hot and spicy (sauce)';
origin_explanation: ETHiOPiC: Te. māqqāra 'be spicy', for which see maq'ara, below.

maq'ara (K*) 'render tasty with salt or vine- gar, soften by soaking'; (M 68 renders it as 'choose, prefer' having misunderstood the Amh. mārāččā 'taste good' [the translation given in T.Y] as if it were mārrāṭā 'choose'); tamaqq'ara (K*), passive; maq'ər, maqqur 'preserved, marinated, soaked'. SEMiTiC: Ar. maqara 'macerate (salt fish)'. Amb. suggests Ar. maqira 'be bitter, be acid'. ETHiOPiC: Te. māqqāra 'be spicy, be tasty', Tna. māqqārā, Amh. māqqārā 'be sweet, be delicious', passed into Cushitic: Sa. moqur, moqūr 'preserved, pickled'. See also maqara.
variant of meṭrā, below.

phonetics: moqar, moqart (pl. mawāqər) ሞቀር, ሞቀርት; see waqara.

origin_explanation: from Ar. maqāriba 'Moors, North Africans'.

phonetics: maq'asa መቅ'ሰ, (T.Y.Sw. in D 1394) moqasa መቅሰ, maqqasa መቅሰ, (MA) moq'asa
maqaṭ መቀት 'cut off, cut up, deform, remove, take away, (T.Y) mutilate, destroy, separate, make small, diminish, break a promise' (note that there is no maqaṭ asa as given in Brockelmann 1908:162);
meaning: cut off, cut up, deform, remove, take away, (T.Y) mutilate, destroy, separate, make small, diminish, break a promise' (note that there is no maqaṭ asa as given in Brockelmann 1908:162);
origin_explanation: ETHiOPiC: Tna. māqəṭā 'cut' (augmented by n) 'tear out', Te. māqəṭaṣa (note that Te. and Tna. mnqṣ, mnqṣ can go back to nqṣ). Amh. māqqaṣā 'cut with scissors, trim (hair)', is a denominative from māqas 'scissors' from Ar. miqaṣṣ 'scissors' (for maqas 'scis- sors' in the other Ethiopian languages, see Leslau 1979: 416). While Ar. miqaṣṣ 'scissors' goes back to the root qṣṣ 'cut', this is not the case with G. maqaṭ asa, as suggested by Dillmann 182, and Arm- bruster 157.

phonetics: mq's, tamaq'asa, tamaqosa መቅ'ስ, ተመቅ'ሰ, ተመቆሰ (T.KBT 425, under qomos) 'become an abbot';
origin_explanation: denominative from mq's, metathesis of qms; see qomos, below.

phonetics: maqsa መቅሰ (T) 'place where cattle spend the night';
meaning: place where cattle spend the night';

origin_explanation: from Ar. maqṭa'a, from qittā'a 'cucumber'. See also qaṭā.

phonetics: maqaṭa I መቀጠ (L); see maraqaṭa, above.
māqoyo ማቆዮ, mawqiyā መውቅያ (Lt) 'kind of illness';
origin_explanation: from Amh. maqoyo 'kind of illness ascribed to the influence of buda and zār-spirits'. For the various Amharic forms, see Streleyn 1955:328. For G. mawqiyā, see Lifchitz, GLECS 3 (1937-40). 84. The noun is derived from Amh. wāqqa 'thresh'.

phonetics: moqay, moqayt (pl. mawāqəy) ሞቀይ, ሞቀይት; see waqaya I.

origin_explanation: SEMiTiC: Ar. qalama 'cut trees, pare (nails)'. ETHiOPiC: G. maqlamt is probably taken from Amh. qəlamt (also wāqlamt); in the other Ethiopian lan- guages: Te. māqəlmāt 'knife', Gur. māqəlant 'passed into Cushitic: Had. maqəlanṭa. Note mt (G.Amh) məqəmt nt, nt (Gur.Had).
see magnitos.
denominative from maqlaz, for which see q°alaza.

phonetics: maqāmər መቃምር, (Lt) əlmaqāmər ዕልመቃምር 'game played with stones' (Amh. əlqa- məṭ);
meaning: game played with stones' (Amh. əlqa- məṭ);
origin_explanation: it consists of throwing up into the air five small stones which must be caught be- fore they fall to the ground; see qumār.

phonetics: maqana መቅነ (T.M) 'be in difficulty, be anx- ious';
maqānīt, pl. (D 449, under qny) 'instruments of torture';
origin_explanation: denominative from maq'an (see qa'ana). Note the loss of ' of maq'an.

phonetics: maqnaṣ (pl. maqānəṣ) መቅነጽ, maqnaṣa መቅነጸ (K*) 'lintel, doorframe';
origin_explanation: metathesis in relation to maq'an (see qa'ana).

phonetics: maqanṭ, maq'anṭ መቅንጥ, መቅ'ንጥ (T), ma- qanṭ መቅንጥ (Sw. in D 1394) 'foot of a bed'.

origin_explanation: see magnitos.

phonetics: maqaqa I መቀቀ (K*) 'wear a coarse woolen cloth, abrade the skin with a coarse cloth (mourner)';
meaning: wear a coarse woolen cloth, abrade the skin with a coarse cloth (mourner)';
'corrupt with lust, debauch, cause to stumble'
origin_explanation: tamarra 'be given to lasciviousness (or debauchery, lust, immodesty)' moru' 'debauched, lascivious, lustious, dissolute' mar'at, mar'at, (T) mar'at 'debauch, ness, indecency, lust, wantonness, in luxury' mar'a, mara', same
it is strange that Amh. wata also means 'leg-irons, shackles' as is the case with maqaqayaš I.
ETHIOPIC: Perhaps Amh. muqat 'thick needle for sewing sacks or mats'.

see Strelcyn 1955:329, Mercier 281, and passim. SEMITIC: J. Grill, ZAW 8 (1888).274-6 states that the basic meaning of mar'a is 'Lustbarkeit, Vergnügen, eine Gelegenheit bei der man sich wohl es üppig hergeht', and connects it with mar'a 'wedding', mar'awi 'bridegroom', originally 'Hochzeit' which, according to him, is not to be derived from r?w. He identifies the Geez root mr' with Ar. mar'a 'abound with herbage' (so also Dillmann 167), mara'a 'anoint (the head)', and Heb. *mere' 'bridegroom's escort, best man, confidential friend'. However, for the origin of the root for 'marry, wedding, bridegroom', see mrw, below. Tna. tamara'a 'get married', Te.Tna. mar'awi 'bridegroom', mar'at 'bride', Amh. marat 'female to whom one is related by marriage', that is, 'daughter-in-law, sister-in-law, mother-in-law'. Gur. mar'a: passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af. mare'a 'wedding'.

meaning: mari, mari, title applied to certain saints or to ecclesiastic dignitaries
origin_explanation: from Aram.Syr. mari; also Chr.-Ar. mar, mari, Tna.Amh. mar. See also maranat.
see marah, marih.
ETHIOPIC: Perhaps Amh. muri, mure 'kind of grass'.

mar'a (T.OT), mar'a (D) 'leather; backpack for carrying a baby (= Amh. anqalba), a wrap-around, (T.D) earthenware tripod on which the cooking pot rests above the fire (that is, the tripod that supports the pot)'; mora' 'mora, mora', pl. mawara't (T), mora' 'mora' (D 889, under *wara') 'waistband, girdle, apron'. (Gr.274 mora'ra is either a misprint for mora' or it expresses the gemination of r; ETHIOPIC: Amh. mir 'leather belt worn by unmarried girls'; also in Cushitic: Sa. moro. See also mora.

meaning: (T) mar'a C 'be lascivious, be lustful, be dissolute, be licentious, be debauched, enjoy venereal pleasure'
origin_explanation: tamarra' 'corrupt with lust, debauch, cause to stumble'; tamara 'be given to lasciviousness (or debauchery, lust, immodesty)'; moru' 'debauched, lascivious, lustious, dissolute'; mar'at, mar'at, (T) mar'at 'debauch, ness, indecency, lust, wantonness, in luxury'; mar'a, mara', same. SEMITIC: J. Grill, ZAW 8 (1888).274-6 states that the basic meaning of mar'a is 'Lustbarkeit, Vergnügen, eine Gelegenheit bei der man sich wohl es üppig hergeht', and connects it with mar'a 'wedding', mar'awi 'bridegroom', originally 'Hochzeit' which, according to him, is not to be derived from r?w. He identifies the Geez root mr' with Ar. mar'a 'abound with herbage' (so also Dillmann 167), mara'a 'anoint (the head)', and Heb. *mere' 'bridegroom's escort, best man, confidential friend'. However, for the origin of the root for 'marry, wedding, bridegroom', see mrw, below. Tna. tamara'a 'get married', Te.Tna. mar'awi 'bridegroom', mar'at 'bride', Amh. marat 'female to whom one is related by marriage', that is, 'daughter-in-law, sister-in-law, mother-in-law'. Gur. mar'a: passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af. mare'a 'wedding'.
metathesis of ma'ar 'honey', with reduplication of the last radical.

amar'awa (K*) 'cause to get married'; mar'awi 'wedding, marriage feast'; mar'awi (pl. mar'aw) 'nuptial, groom, son-in-law'; (bar'ala mar'awi [Lt] 'wedding'); mar'awi (pl. mar'aw) 'bride, daughter-in-law, spouse'; mar'awanna 'state of being a bride or bridegroom'; tamar'awi (Gr.157) 'man who gets married' denominative from mara'a from ra'awa 'join, yoke'. Dillmann 309 (followed by Nyberg 231) connects it with Heb. re' 'friend'; cp. also Akk. ru'u.

perhaps lit. 'donkey caught in a snare' (for 'snare', see maraba, below). KWK 818, under rababa, renders marab with 'desert, uninhabited place', that is, a place in which one sets up snares for animals; adga marab would then mean 'the donkey of an uninhabited place'. Gr. 84 interprets marab as the name of the river Mareb, and adga marab would then be 'the donkey of (the region of) Mareb'.

meaning: 'set traps, lay snares, catch fish'; marab (T.M) 'trap, snare'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. marraba 'cast a net'; Tna.Amh. marab, marbab 'fisherman's net'; see rbb I, marbabt, and marab I.

M. Cohen, in Mélanges Louis Massignon, vol.1 (1956).327 suggests a connection with marabou 'stork', and states "ce qui pourrait représenter le transfer d'une dénomination peu connue à un fauve répandu"; cp. also Amh. marabu 'marabou stork'.

meaning: 'leap, hasten, walk fast, run about, rush in, attack, (TS 118) bother, annoy'
origin_explanation: amarrada (Gr.86), causative; 'whitewash'; tamarrada (Gr.86), pass. of maraga; marragi (Gr.87), act. part. of maraga; marrug 'plastered, cemented together'; marg 'marg, marag', (K.T) marag 'plaster, white lime, coating, (Lt) morass'; margat 'plastering' (n). SEMITIC: Ar. maraga 'mix'; related to mrq (Heb. Aram.Syr.Md) 'polish'. Reinsch 1887:274 identifies it with the Semitic roots mrh, mrh, mrg 'rub, be smear'.
marradi (K), act. part. of marrada; marrud (K*) 'who is attacked'; marrad, marrad 'uproar, rushing (n.adj), race, assault, attack, battle, persecution, raid'.

marrad (Lt) 'footrace'. SEMITIC: Ar. marrad 'be rebellious'; Heb. marad 'rebel, revolt'. Aram. Syr. marad, Md. mrd. ETHIOPIC: Tna. marrad 'incursion, eruption', Amh. marrada 'hasten, raid, pillage'. See also mardada II.

meaning: 'be obstinate, be courageous, be audacious, be daring, (K*) be assiduous, be tenacious; (T.Y)= Amh. dafana 'bury, block', probably a misprint for dafara 'be courageous'; mardud 'obstinate, tenacious, audacious'; (M.Sw. in D 170 wrongly renders it by an abstract 'obstinacy, assiduity', for which see marded)'
origin_explanation: marded (T) 'obstinacy, tenacity' (the Amharic translation dafnat 'burying' is a misprint for dafrat); so also Sw. in D 170. the verb seems to be a denominative from mardud or marded, from rdd, below. ETHIOPIC: Amh. mardud 'bold' (from Geez).
to be identified with marrada, above, with reduplication of the last radical.
one recognizes dartiqun, for which see darteqon 'the second heaven'.

amarraga (Gr.86), causative; 'whitewash'; tamarraga (Gr.86), pass. of maraga; marragi (Gr.87), act. part. of maraga; marrug 'plastered, cemented together'; marg, marg, marag, (K.T) marag 'plaster, white lime, coating, (Lt) morass'; margat 'plastering' (n). SEMITIC: Ar. maraga 'mix'; related to mrq (Heb. Aram.Syr.Md) 'polish'. Reinsch 1887:274 identifies it with the Semitic roots mrh, mrh, mrg 'rub, be smear'.

meaning: Tna.Gur. märägäḍä 'plaster', Te. märägäḍ, Amh. märägäḍä, Har. märäqä;
origin_explanation: passed into Cushitic Bil. marq, Or. maraqa.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. märägbo 'aardwolf'. See also di-rägbo.
marägd (Lt.MK) 'ceremonial dance performed by the priests'; the verb is a denominative from marägd going back to ragada, below.

meaning: 'slap hard, accompany a hymn with sistrum and drum', amäräggäḍä 'dance (the däbṭära) while chanting a hymn to the accompa-niment of drum and sistrum', Tna. märägḍī 'chant-ing of a hymn accompanied by dancing' ETHIOPIC: Amh. märäggäḍä 'slap hard, accompany a hymn with sistrum and drum', amäräggäḍä 'dance (the däbṭära) while chanting a hymn to the accompa-niment of drum and sistrum', Tna. märägḍī 'chant-ing of a hymn accompanied by dancing'.

origin_explanation: (M 56 quoting Tayyä translates it 'ornamento di marmo fatto a ombrello', but this meaning does not occur in Tayyä); (əbna maragd 'emerald'); from Gk. maragdos (rare) = smaragdos 'emerald'; itic: Bil. marh, Sa.Af. marah. The connection with Be. melah 'guide' suggested by Bittner, WZKM 23 (1909).146 is doubtful.

meaning: 'amar-gaḥa 'roll (stones), throw (stone)'; margaḥ (pl. marägoḥ) 'rock, stone, (T) boulder'; mərguḥ, mərguḥ (Gr.87) 'rolled'; amargäḥi, amargäḥi (Gr.87) 'who rolls (stones)';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. märägaḥ 'boulder', Amh. märg; pas-sed into Cushitic: Qem. märg. See also mrg.

meaning: 'use a fan, winnow (by blowing or fanning)'
origin_explanation: the form with the vowel e after the m is strange.

meaning: 'lean upon a staff, lean on (a person), rely'
origin_explanation: marg'az (pl. maräg'az) 'staff, rod, cane'; marguz (K*) 'who leans upon a staff'; SEMITIC: Mh. markez 'rod, staff (Bittner 1909:31). ETHIOPIC: Amh. (ṭä)märäg'azä, (ṭä)märäkk'azä 'lean on a stick', Gur. (ṭä)märäk'azä, Tna. (ṭä)märäk'asä, Har. (at)rēgäza 'support', Te. mər-kuz, mərkus 'stick', Tna. mərkus, mərk'as Amh. märg'az; passed into Cushitic: Sa. markos 'staff to lean upon', Qem. mərkuz.
see mariḥ.

'amraḥa, causative; tamaraḥa 'be guided, be led'; maräḥi (pl. maräḥäyān) 'who er, catechist, leader of a choir'; mərūḥ (Gr.83) 'guided, led'; marrāḥ (K*) 'guide, leader'; marḥ (pl. amraḥt; also amha 'instructor'; (amraḥt, pl. of marḥ 'guidance'; marḥat 'guidance'; məmräḥ (Gr.83) 'place where tamaraḥi 'who is guided, who one who repeats the songs after the maräḥi' (Velat 687); tamarḥo 'a'wäf 'augury', lit. 'the being guided by birds'; ETHIOPIC: Te. märḥa 'guide, lead', Tna. märḥe, Arg. märraḥa, Amh. märra, Gur. māra; passed into Cush-itic: Bil. marh, Sa.Af. maraḥ. The connection with Be. melah 'guide' suggested by Bittner, WZKM 23 (1909).146 is doubtful.
from hora 'go' (see hwr), with metathesis.
metathesis of maräwəḥt (from rwḥ I).
from Ar. mirrīḥ 'Mars (planet)'; also Amh. märiḥ. See also mari, maräḥ.
From Ar. markab; also Te.Tna.Amh.Har.Gur. märkāb.

meaning: the eighth month of the Coptic calendar (corresponds to G. miyazayā)
origin_explanation: variant of barmudä; from Coptic parmoute through Chr.Ar. barmūda.
from Gk. marmaros 'marble'; also Ar. marmar, Syr. marmoron.
SEMITIC: if the original form is marmale (see above), the root could be rml, and cp. Aram. tarmīlā 'bag', Syr. tarmālā. ETHIOPIC: Amh. märmäre 'undergarment, undershirt'.
corruption of Gk. myrmēkoleōn 'ant-lion' (Hommel 1877:67).
possibly a denominative from *mrmt, from rml, and cp. Tna. rämṭäṭä 'break to pieces', Te. rämṭäṭä.
from Syr. mārān, Aram. mārānā. See also mārānāt.
marana seems to be reconstructed from maränāt, metathesis of manärät, above.

meaning: 'chrism (made from oil of special plants), sweet oil, holy oil' (see Walker 8-9)
origin_explanation: from Gk. myron 'sweet oil' (also used for holy oil); also Tna.Amh. meron, Ar. mīrūn, Chr.-Ar. mayrūn, mīrūn, Syr. mīrōn, mūrōn; Som. mayrūn, from Arabic.
see mär; Dillmann 163, under mär, derives it from Aram. mārānā tä 'our Lord, come!' through Gk. maranatha.

SEMITIC: Ar. maraq 'broth', Heb. māraq. ETHIOPIC: Te.Amh.Arg.Gaf.Gur. märāq 'broth', Tna. märāq, Har. märāx (< *märāq < märāg). It is possible that Ethiopian märāq is borrowed from Ar. maraq. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af.Som. maraq, Or. maraqi.
see also masriqu.
gives the impression of being a transcription from another language.
ETHIOPIC: for the meaning 'bolt for a door', cp. Tna. räq'e 'fasten, attach with nails, bolt'.

meaning: = Amh. yämure an-gärä 'bread made from the seeds of murā-gärä'
origin_explanation: in the initial murā one recognizes Amh. mure 'grass used for making whisks for cleaning a grinding slab'.

meaning: 'skin, peel, strip'; marqah (T) 'butcher' (it has the form of a plural);
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. märqäḥa 'split, cut, break into splinters', Amh. märqäḥ 'butcher' (from Geez). The noun marqäḥ could possibly be derived from raqha I, below, and marqäḥa would then be a denomina-tive.
mrqʿaṭo (T) 'letting (n) the hair grow, full head of long hair, (Sw. in D 1393) tuft of feathers'; ETHIOPIC: Amh. märäqqʿaṭä 'become bushy (hair that has not been cut)'. See also mərqʿaṭ.
sär from Amh. sar 'grass'. Could mərqʿaṭ be connected with mərqʿ?

meaning: 'be bitter, be embittered, be grieved, be provoked'
origin_explanation: tamara 'embitter, grieve, provoke, affect deeply, exasperate, become embittered, (T) deprive, hold in low esteem'; tamarra (Gr.83) 'be bitter'; mərər 'bitter, irritated, exasperated'; marir (fem. marrär) 'bitter, something bitter, embittered, ferocious, provoking, sorrowful, grievous'; (marira 'bitterly'); (beta marirän 'a rebellious house', transcription of Heb. bet məri); marärä (Lt) 'violent, vehement'; mərär 'bitterness, affliction, rancor, acerbity, (Lt) strong potion'; marrat 'bitterness, anger, rancor, rebellion'; ʿamrärä 'that makes bitter'; also part. of ʿamrara; SEMITIC: Ar. marra 'be bitter', Mh. marr, Heb. mar (mrr), Aram. mərar 'be bitter, grieve', Syr. mar, Md. mrr, Akk. marâru. For 'gall bladder, bile' from the root mrr, cp. Ar. mirra, Heb. mərerâ, Aram. məreräṭa, Syr. merîṭa, Md. mirta, mirîrta, martu, Akk. martu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. märärä 'be bitter', Te. märra, Amh. märärä, Arg. märära, Har. märära, Gur. märärä. For 'bile', cp. Har. mərar, Arg. märara. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. marir 'bitter', Bil. märär, Or. marar 'be sad', Ka. mirâro 'bile'.

meaning: 'be putrefied, be spoiled, become soft or tender, be softened by sleeping, be worm-eaten, smell'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. marata, marasa 'steep food in water', Mh. mirôs 'dissolve', Pbh. mrs (pʿel) 'stir with a liquid', Aram.Syr. maras 'steep, make soft by steep-ing', Md. mrs 'bruise, squash', Ug. mrt 'moisten' (Aistleitner), Akk. marâšu 'stir with a liquid'. ETHIOPIC: Te. märsa 'boil' (Littmann-Höfner 113), Amh. marräsä 'become soft (soil, by having manure dumped on it)'.

phonetics: marsəne, marsäni, mirasəns, mersəns, mərsənən, marsinen (OT) marsonəm (T*)
origin_explanation: from Gk. myrsinē, gen. myrsinēs, acc. myrsinēn 'myrtle'. Also Ar. marsin, Amh. marsənə. See also bərsəna not.

meaning: 'grope for, feel the way, search (for something)'
origin_explanation: ʿamarsasa, causative; (H 226 wrongly renders Amh. dabbäsäṣa [translation of ʿamarsasa] with 'be tarnished'); tamarsasa (Gr.84), passive; marsas 'groping, feeling the way' (B 44 wrongly renders Amh. mädäsäs [translation of G. marsas] with 'flattery, cajoling'); marsäsi (Gr.84), act. part. of marsasa; root augmented by r in relation to mss of Semitic and Ethiopic. SEMITIC: Ar. massa (mss) 'touch', SAR. msṣ, Mh. môsî 'rub', Sh. miss 'touch', Heb. msš (pʿel) 'feel through, search, grope', Aram. məšas, Syr. mas (mšš) 'touch, feel', Md. mšš, Akk. mašâšu 'wipe, polish'. The root is related to mws in Heb.Aram. Syr. mas 'feel, touch', Md. muš. ETHIOPIC: Tna. masäsä, masäwä 'rub', Te. mässa (mss), masäsa 'wipe', Amh. mʿassäsä 'rub', Har. mäsa 'rub, wipe', Gur. mʿasä, məsäššä. See also ramsasa.
ʿamarsasa (Lt) 'reach land'; denominative from marso, mars, above, with reduplication of the last radical. See also marsawa.
ʿamarsawa (Gr.84), causative; marsəw (Gr.84) 'tranquil, quiet, calm'; the verb is a denominative from marso, above. See also marsasa II.
is a misprint for maqras.
tamärata 'consult a fortune-teller, soothsayer, practice divination'; denominative from a noun *mrt from Amh. (a)mʿarräṭä 'prophesy, foretell, cast a spell to injure someone'.
ʿamrata (K*), causative; denominative from maret, below; also Tna.Amh. märräṭä 'become soil'.

meaning: 'earth, dust of the ground, soil, clay, plaster, dung'
origin_explanation: (maret ṣaʿadä 'plaster, clay'); mareṭawi 'creature of the earth or of earthly, being (n) of dust'; SEMITIC: Sar. mrt-m 'limestone' (?); ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh. märet 'earth, ground'. See also marata II.
SEMITIC: Ar. maraṭa 'pull out', Heb. maraṭ (hair)', Aram.Syr. maraṭu 'scrape, scratch off'. The root is related to malaṭa. ETHIOPIC: Har. märäṭa 'strip off', Gur. märäṭä.
from Amh. märräṭä 'choose', in the other Ethiopian languages: Tna. märäṣä, Te. märṣa 'be shining'. Arg. märäṭa 'choose'.

martuläwi, martəläwi 'pertaining to a tabernacle'; represents martur, below, with alternance of liquids. W. Müller compares early North Arabic martûl 'Martyrion (as a building)' which occurs in an Arabic inscription of Harrân in 568.
(ṭaboṭ zamartur 'ark of the testimony'); (dabtära zamartur 'tabernacle of the testimony'); from Gk. martyrion ('religious) testimony, shrine of a martyr'. See also martul, above.
denominative from Amh. märo 'chisel'.

meaning: 'place cross-pieces in building a house wall'
origin_explanation: maräwi (DTW 749, under magari), act. participle; marw, ib. 'wall of poles fastened to the ground'; ETHIOPIC: Tna. märäwä 'furnish with a trellis', Te. mawära (with metathesis) 'tie together', märo 'ring of willow branches used in the roof of a round hut'.

meaning: 'a wooden tool serving for putting collyrium into the eyes'
origin_explanation: from Ar. mirwad, pl. marâwid 'ivory needle for applying collyrium'. See also marwad.
from Amh. märäwwät. See also (rwh II), marwäht.

meaning: act as a soothsayer, divine, practice divination, perform magic
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Heb.Aram. mwr (hif'il), hemir 'exchange'. Ambros suggests, with reservations, a connection with Ar. marra 'pass by'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. Marya 'leap year'.

meaning: water for the gäbäta-bowl serving for washing the feet
multicolored, imported cloth placed on a monarch's head at coronation
origin_explanation: Guidi, ZA 11 (1896). 405 makes an attempt to derive it from damas 'Damascus', known for various kinds of fabric. ETHIOPIC: Amh. mäs, same.

meaning: joining of beams or rafters, panels, floor, story, lining, paneled ceiling
origin_explanation: metathesis of mas'art, from 'asara 'bind'. See also mas'art, below.
to be identified with mas'art (see sǝ'art).

meaning: north, north wind, southwest wind, southeast
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. mis' 'north wind', Mh. msa' 'south'. For the identity between Ar. mis' and nis', see P. Haupt, AJSL 22 (1905-6). 199, and Noeldeke 1910:62. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Te.Amh. mäsə' 'northeast'.

meaning: basket, platter, basket for the Eucharist
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Arg.Gaf.Gur. mäsob; also in Cushitic: Or. masobi, Bil. mäsob. The derivation from a root *swb (Ar. täba, Heb. šub 'return'), matäb 'place where people gather' suggested by Praetorius 1879:159, n.1 is not convincing since matäb means 'place at which someone appears or returns' rather than 'gathers'. Likewise the connection with Te. säfä, säfi, säfi, säfi 'plate of palm wattle for refining corn' suggested, with reservations, by Rundgren 1955:44 is unlikely. Akk. masabbu 'basket' is probably accidentally similar.
(M 59 quotes wrongly masäbehe); could it be a misunderstanding of Ar. masäbäḥ 'rosaries' (pl. of misbaḥa)?

meaning: bunch of grapes or corn when not yet ripe

origin_explanation: musd probably comes from mawsad, from soda (swd) 'scratch, scrape'; in this case massada would be a denominative from mawsad, musd. Note that Y renders massada by Amh. ቍርጥ ቍርጥätä 'brew beer or mead for sale'. It is strange that massada ቍርጥ is rendered in T.Y. by Amh. ቍርጥ šämmätä 'deal in grain'. In all likelihood massada is another spelling for massada, and šämmätä is either misspelled for ቍርጥ mäššätä, or the other way around.
is a misprint for masbǝt, masbät ቍርጥ from šäbäta.
denominative from masfǝn; see safana.
DTW 1200 explains it through Amh. yäsäfa 'heap (or 'quality') of meat'; see safara I.
K 614 identifies it with G. 'asfäras 'jaundice', above.
from safaya 'sew', probably developed into the meaning 'tie'.
from GK. mosphaitham, transcription of Heb. miṣpa tayim. See also mosopetam.
see sahaga I.
see malaga II.

from Ar. masğid; also Te. mäsgäd, Amh. mäsgid, Har. mäsgid. In this connection the origin of mäsgid 'house of prayer' used by the Falashas is of interest. The generally accepted origin of the Falasha mäsgid as coming from Ar. masğid is surprising. W. Müller drew my attention to a South Arabian inscription coming from a Jewish house of prayer which reads, 'Amen, amen, and this house is the msgd-n' (AO 24[1973].154-5). While the SAr. msgd is taken from Aramaic, the Falasha mäsgid goes back to SAr. msgd-n and is not borrowed from Ar. masğid. As for Ar. masğid, it is also taken from Aramaic, through the intermediary of SAr. msgd-n.

meaning: 'faith of the Unctionists'; see Hammerschmidt, in Assfalg-Krüger 60); masihānnā 'messiahship, function of Messiah';
'drag, draw, bend (a bow), distend, render tight or taut by pulling, (Y) straighten up, (T) bring near'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. masaka 'aroint', Sar. h-ms.h, Sh. mes.h 'rub, stroke', Heb. māsah 'anoint', Aram.-Syr. mǝš-āh, Md. mšā (mšh), Ug. mšh, Akk. mušu'u 'rub'. For the Aram.-Syr. origin of the G. and Ar. masih, masih, see Noeldeke 1910:34, Jeffery 265. ETHIOPIC: Tna. masho 'fresh butter used for greasing the hair', Amh. (a)m"assā 'oil a new griddle many times before trying to bake bread on it' (Getatchew). The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. mišo 'butter'. See also masha II.

meaning: 'feast, eat a midday meal or dinner'.
origin_explanation: 'amsāha, causative; also 'invite for a meal, prepare a meal'; tamasha (K*), passive of masha; masāhi, act. part. of masha; masāh 'meal, midday meal, dinner, supper, banquet, love-feast of the early Christians, feast which a particular zar-spirit is believed to crave' (Amh. mas); (masāha dabra šəyon [Gr.88] 'the banquet on Mount Zion'; see Getatchew, Paideuma 29 [1983],108-9); məmsāh (M) 'place of a banquet in a refectory'. SEMITIC: Dillmann 176 derives G. məsāh 'meal' from Semitic and Ethiopic msh 'smear, anoint' (see masha I), the action referring to the smearing of the prepared dishes with fat. The Ethiopic root msh (G. məsāh 'meal') is not to be compared with Mh. fse 'breakfast', as suggested by Bittner 1909:20. For Mh. fse, see Brockelmann 1927:18. ETHIOPIC: Te. məsāh 'meal, breakfast', Tna. masah, Amh.Arg. masa, also in Cushitic: Qem. mezi, Kham. mesā, Bil. medā (with alternance d::z,s), Or. misi, Mo. mišo.

meaning: 'drag, draw, bend (a bow), distend, render tight or taut by pulling, (Y) straighten up, (T) bring near'.
origin_explanation: 'amāsaka (Gr.90), causative; tamaska (Gr.90, D 1427), passive of masaka; masāki (Gr.90), act. part. of masaka; masuk (Gr.90), pass. part. of masaka; SEMITIC: Ar. masaka 'grab, grasp', Heb. māšak 'drag, draw', Aram. mašak. ETHIOPIC: Amh. mässākā 'pull, tug', Gur. māsākā 'pull'; possibly Te. māsaka 'saddle, harness' (Littmann-Hofner 120); passed into Cushitic: Had. ma-sakkā-kko 'pull, drag', Sid. massagi. See also ma-saka II.
could be an extension of the meaning 'bend a bow stretching > make thin' (see masaka I).
from Ar. misk < Pers. mušk; also Amh. mask, Syr. mušk (see Jeffery 264).

meaning: (L) 'be poor' (denominative); tamaskana 'become poor'; 'amaskana (Gr.91) 'cause to be poor'; maskin (fem. maskint, maskənt) 'poor, wretched, needy'; maskinat 'poverty, indigence'.
origin_explanation: from Aram.-Syr. misken 'poor' going back to Akka-dian muškēnu (von Soden 684); also Ar. miskin. For a bibliography on this root, see Wagner 80. ETHIOPIC: Tna. mäskin, mäskīn 'poor', mäskīnā, mäskīnā 'become poor', Te. mäskana, Amh. mäskin 'poor', mäskīnnā 'become poor'.
from Amh. mäskärä; also in Tna. mäskärä.

meaning: first Ethiopian month (from September 11 to October 10).
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh.Gur. mäskäräm; also in Cush-itic: Qab. mäskäräma, Kam. mosorōma. Dillmann 835 derives mäskäräm from krm 'rainy season'.
from Ar. mish, considered by Vollers 1896:649 of Persian (māšu, mašōga) origin.

meaning: (T.M) 'covering for a corpse, covering of the tābot-ark'.
origin_explanation: from Ar. mishal 'thin garment, piece of cloth'.

meaning: proverbs' (most of these meanings are denomi-natives either from məsl or massālē);
origin_explanation: Praetorius 1879:158 likewise connects māsəkarām with krm, but corrects it to māsəkarām, an active participle of the as-stem. However, his translation 'that which brings rain' is not appropriate to the weather conditions in Ethiopia, since māsəkarām marks the end of the rainy season. Conti Rossini 1923:375-6 is aware of the difficulty and renders māsəkarām 'that which makes the rainy season end'. The form, however, is māsəkarām and not mäskäräm.

masəkəw (Lt) 'plants'; SEMITIC: Dillmann 382 derives G. maskot from the Semitic root *skw (Aram. səke 'look out, note also PbH. sāqā 'look'), but this root is not represented in any of the Ethiopian languages. That Ar. miškāt 'window' is considered a G. word (Noeldeke 1910:51, Jeffery 266; see also Dozy 1:780). ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. mäskot 'window'; passed into Cushitic: Sa. maskot. Rabin 123, n. 20 suggests that "the Ethiopic word was borrowed from Hijazi Arabic, and the latter took it from Aramaic of the Arabian Jews who might have used the Hebrew maskīt".
seems to be influenced by Amh. amäsäkkä"a. For the Geez form, see msk".

meaning: (T.M) 'assign land to a monas-tery, found a monastery'.
origin_explanation: Since Amh. gaddämä (the translation of given by Tayyä) also means 'raise a church gädäm (monastery) status so that it may sanctuary', maskaya is to be considered a dena-tive from maskāy 'refuge, asylum', from saka I.

meaning: 'be like, look like, be likened to, resemble, appear, seem'; with object suffix pronouns it means 'it seems to someone (kama 'that'), thin, sup-pose'.
origin_explanation: 'amsala 'declare similar, declare alike, make believe, regard as, hold as equivalent, consider someone so and so, think, compare, believe'; 'amassala (Gr.87), caus. of masla; tamasla, tamassala 'be made similar, resemble, look like, be like, be likened, be similar, imi-tate, emulate, be compared, compare oneself, be equal to, take the appearance of, disguise oneself as, transform oneself, appear as, be formed in the likeness of, give the impression of (being), pretend, simulate, imitate'; tamāsāla 'resemble one another, be compared to one another, be made equal to one another, be equal to one another, be like one another, resemble'; 'astamasala 'use a pretense, compare'; 'astamāsāla 'liken, liken with one another, compare, compare with one another, repre-sent, consider as similar, make similar to one another, make believe, put forth a parable'; masāli, massāli (pl. massāläyān), act. part. of masla, massala; massəl 'formed'; masl (pl. masal, maslāt, 'amsāl, 'amsālāt) 'like-ness, similarity, representation, form, figure, image, statue, parable, proverb'; 'amsāl (plural of masl, but having also the meaning of a singular) 'aspect, form, figure, effigy, image, likeness, like thing, similarity, similitude, model, symbol, analogy, imitation, description, parable, proverb'; ('amsāla, ba-'amsāla 'like, in the manner of, in the guise of, by way of, kind of'); masla 'with, in the company of, in addition to'; also used with verbs expressing reciprocity; ac-cording to the context it also has the meanings of other prepositions; e.g. talelayu 'ahadu masla kāla'u 'they were separated each from his brother (friend, another)', 'akko kama takāt gassu maslehu 'his countenance was not toward him as before'; (masla zəntu k'allu 'besides all this, for all, nonetheless'); with suffix pronouns the base is masle-; məssālē (pl. massāläyāt) 'similitude, compari-son, parable, proverb, example';

meaning: 'that which is similar, similitude, likeness, type'
'who emulates, similar, comparable, made similar, represented'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. matala 'be like', SAR. mtl 'the like of, similar to', Heb. mšl (nif-al) 'be likened', Aram. Syr. mətal 'compare, liken', Md. mtl, Akk. mašālu. For məšl 'parable', cp. Ar. matal, Heb. māšāl, Aram. mətlā, Syr. mətlā, Md. mtila. ETHIOPIC: Tna. māšālā 'be like', Te. māšla, Amh.Gaf. māššālā, Arg. māššālā, Har. māššālā, Gur. māšālā; passed into Cushitic: Sa. masal 'be like'.
From Ar. muslim. Also in Te. 'amāslama 'become a Muslim', məsləm 'Muslim', Tna. māslāmā 'become a Muslim', 'aslamay 'Muslim', Amh. muslim. See also 'aslām.
From Ar. muslim. Also in Te. 'amāslama 'become a Muslim', məsləm 'Muslim', Tna. māslāmā 'become a Muslim', 'aslamay 'Muslim', Amh. muslim. See also 'aslām.

meaning: 'decay, be spoiled, be corrupt, deteriorate, become rotten, be made desolate, be laid waste, be ruined, be abolished, be destroyed, perish'
origin_explanation: (zayamāssan 'corruptible, destructible, perishable'); (zaviyyəmāssən 'incorruptible, indestructible, imperishable'); 'amāsana 'spoil, ruin, corrupt, pervert, subvert, deteriorate, demolish, destroy, lay waste, devastate, make desolate, wipe out'; tamāsana 'be corrupted, be laid waste'; māsāni, act. part. of māsana; also 'perishable, corruptible'.

meaning: 'corrupt, decayed, spoilt, destroyed, desolate, perishable, damaged, putrid, rotten, withering'
origin_explanation: musənt (Lt) 'ruin'.

meaning: 'spoiling (n), rotting (n), corruption, perdition, ruin, desvasta-tion, destruction, depravity, perversion'
origin_explanation: 'imusənnā 'incorruptibility'; 'amāsāni, part. of 'amāsana; mamāsāni, (Gr) mamāsāni, same.

SEMITIC: the derivation from Semitic wšn (Heb. yāšān 'old') with secondary m suggested by Dillmann 177 and Noeldeke 1910:20 could be possible only if one considered māsana a denominative from masənnā going back to wšn; however, this develop-ment is not very likely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. masənā 'rot, become thin, be emaciated', Te. masāna 'look sickly', Amh. masənā 'be-come exhausted'; passed into Cushitic: Qem. māsān 'become skinny, perish'.

From Ar. masnuww 'irrigated', Ar. (Hadr) masnā 'irrigation channel'; also Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. māsno, Har. māšnu 'field or garden that is accessible for irrigation'; in Cushitic: Som. masno 'irrigation chan-nel', Or. māšnu. Note that W. Müller, ZDMG 131 (1981). 401 thinks that G. masno derives from *mas-naw and is of Geez origin.

From Ar. masnuww 'irrigated', Ar. (Hadr) masnā 'irrigation channel'; also Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. māsno, Har. māšnu 'field or garden that is accessible for irrigation'; in Cushitic: Som. masno 'irrigation chan-nel', Or. māšnu. Note that W. Müller, ZDMG 131 (1981). 401 thinks that G. masno derives from *mas-naw and is of Geez origin.
see masfetam.
Dillmann 177 states that he found məsuq in two manuscripts, but that in a third manuscript the form is nəsuq (see nasaqa). K 657 considers nəsuq the correct form.
Dillmann 177 states that he found məsuq in two manuscripts, but that in a third manuscript the form is nəsuq (see nasaqa). K 657 considers nəsuq the correct form.

meaning: 'mark with a cross, make the sign of the cross'
origin_explanation: tamasqala (Gr) 'be blessed with the sign of the cross'.
The verb is a denominative from masqal, for which see saqala. The denominative also occurs in Tna. 'amāsəqālā 'lay crosswise', Amh. (tä)māsəqālā 'be placed crosswise, assume a crosswise position'.

meaning: 'weaver's beam, roller on a loom for rolling up the finished cloth, (T) cuirass'
origin_explanation: From Gk. mesaklon 'weaver's beam' (1 Kings 17:7 = 1 Samuel 17:7).

meaning: 'affair of the angels, that which pertains to angels'
origin_explanation: could it be an interpretation of masqalon above?
(the original of MA is məšlč p.107, bottom); SEMITIC: K 614 connects it with Heb. māsār 'de-liver'; if this is the case, it is a transcription of Heb. māsār rather than a cognate.
see mazr.

ETHIOPIC: Amh.Arg.Gaf.Gur. massər 'len-til', Misir. Te. mānsir is probably from Sa. mānsir while Som.Qua. misir, Or. misira, Kham. misir are taken from Semitic Ethiopic. M. Cohen suggests a Sanskrit (masūra) origin.

meaning: (D.T.K), name of the twelfth Coptic month (corresponds to G. nahāse)
origin_explanation: from Coptic mesōrē, mesurē; also Chr.-Ar. misrā.
see also merqu.

the meaning 'large rectangular house' is strange. Tayyā considered perhaps the word for 'wardrobe' to mean 'the place where the wardrobe and other items are stored', that is, 'the house'. As for (OT) 'servant', it refers undoubtedly to the one who is in charge of the wardrobe; from Gk. mesthaad, transcription of Heb. meštaḥat in 4 Kings 10:22 = 2 Kings 10:22 (Dillmann 1400).
either corrupt from Gk. stomakhos 'stomach', or from Gk. mastaks 'mouth, jaw' wrongly interpreted as 'stomach'.
from Heb. mastemā 'animosity'; also in Amh. māste-ma; see Worrell III:139, Euringer, ZS 7 (1929). 74, Dobberahn 107, 177, n.83.
from Gk. mystagōgia 'initiation into the mysteries'; also in Chr.-Ar. mastagūgīya, mastagūgīya (see Lifehitz 71ff).

meaning: 'write in mysteries, speak mysteriously, hide as a secret' (denomina-tive)
origin_explanation: tamastara, passive; also 'be kept secret'; tamastatara (K*) 'share a secret, exchange secrets'

meaning: melt (tr), liquefy, dilute, dissolve, make faint, make pine away
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (ta)māsawa ‘melt’ (Dozy II 593) related to Ar. māta (mwt) ‘dilute’, Ar. (Hadr) mawnwasa ‘melt’, Sh. (msy), muši ‘melt, dissolve’, Heb. msy (hif‘il) ‘dissolve’ (related to mss, nif‘al ‘melt’), Aram.-Syr. masā (msw) ‘melt, waste, drip away’. Dietrich 299 compares Akk. mesū, masū‘u ‘wash’, Md. msa.
from Gk. mesē ‘middle’ (adj), fem. of mesos; also Syr. mesē ‘the middle’.
misprint for masəwas, or the other way around.

meaning: implement for producing fire, inflammable material, fire pan, fire shovel
origin_explanation: (L) gives it the same meaning as maswad; the connection with Heb. sal ‘basket’ suggested by Wajnberg 1937a:29 is unlikely.
it is in the form of a plural.
SEMITIC: Ambros suggests, with reservations, Ar. ma-

meaning: pillow, on account of the shape
origin_explanation: sâwər, pl. of miswar ‘leather pillow’.
see also masəwā.

(masya ‘arb. masya bəhər ‘become evening’, see also sbh I); ‘amsaya ‘spend the evening’; masyat ‘evening, twilight’. SEMITIC: Ar. masā (msw) ‘become evening’, ‘amsi ‘yesterday’. Soq. imšin ‘evening’, Heb. ʽemeš ‘yesterday evening’, Akk. mūšu ‘night’, and explains the ending through -li of where it is an element of the root). ETHIOPIC: Tna. māsāyā ‘become evening’, Te. māsa, Amh. māššā, Arg. māšša, Har. maset ‘evening’, cp. Te. maset, Tna.Gur. məšāt, Gaf. māšātā, Arg. mused, Amh.Har. məšet; perhaps common with Cushitic: Be. amas.
should be masyāh, and see sayha I.

from Gk. messiās, from Aram. masīḥā; also Tna. Amh. masəyas.

see šaʾaya. See also misy.

denominative from masʾe, above.

tamaskʾa, passive; also ‘chew the cud’.

meaning: ruminate, Tna. māskʾa ‘(ani-mal) that ruminates’ (with metathesis: ʾaməkʾəse, ʾamkʾəse).
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. māsəkʾa ‘ruminate’, Tna. māskʾa ‘(ani-mal) that ruminates’ (with metathesis: ʾaməkʾəse, ʾamkʾəse). Amh. (a)mānəzāzāha, (a)mānəzāzāha, (a)mānəzāzāha, Gur. mənzāzāha, mizzākā, məsāxā. The connection with Ar. ḡasara ‘ruminate’ suggested by Praetorius 1890:22 presents the basis of the various Gurage forms, the root seems to be *mzk > msk (by assimilation of zk > sk), > mnzk (a triradical in Gurage) > mnzk (augmented by n). It is also possible to consider msk as the basic root > mzk; mzk by dissimilation (sk > zk). For more details on the Gurage root, see Leslau 1979:413. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Qua. māsək ‘chew’, Qua. masek. See also mskw.
see šāqaya.
see šaraya I.
(pl. mawāšərt); see wašara.

ʾamrāta, causative; tamāsrata, passive of masrāta; masrāti (Gr.89), act. part. of masrāta; masrut, pass. part. of masrāta; masārat, (L) masarat ‘foundation’; the verb is a denominative from masārat, is derived from šārara, below. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sārātā ‘lay a foundation’ (denominative), sārat, māsarrāt ‘foundation’, Te. māsra. Amh.Gur. māsrat. For the G. denominative masrāta, cp. Tna. māsərātā ‘lay a foundation’, Te. māsərātā, Amh.Gur. məsərātā.

meaning: tear to pieces, tear away, cut up, cut up, carry off, carry away, grab, seize off by force, snatch away, kidnap, violently, ravish, enrapture, entrance
origin_explanation: ʾamsāta, (T) ʾamsāta ‘escape, flee, free oneself’, tamāsata, tamāsata, pass. of masāta; ʾamāsata ‘draw oneself’; tamāsəra ‘bring to one another’; ʾastamāsəʾa ‘adjoin, place one thing on top of another, (T) pull, draw’; also ‘rapacious, ravenous, violent, robber, (Lt) usurper’; masūl, pass. part. of masāta; masīt ‘robbery, piracy’; masītat, verbal noun of masāta; mamsāt, (Lt) mamsīl, pl. mamsīl. (Gr.92) mamsāt ‘means of seizing or carrying: handle, loops, staves, (curtain) ring, ringlet, hook, key’. ETHIOPIC: Amh. māsātā ‘snatch away, enrapture, enchant’, Te. māsta ‘delight, enrapture, enchant’, Tna. māsātā ‘carry off by force, enrapture, amaze’. For ‘capture, captivate, fascinate’, cp. Amh. marrākā. Note that several meanings of mamsāt, such as ‘loop, (curtain) ring, key’ could be compared with mamsāt ‘buttons and buttonholes’. It is possible that mamsāt is a variant spelling of mamsat.
from Amh. məst.
see šwr.

meaning: name of the sixth Coptic month (corresponds to G. yakkāti)
origin_explanation: from Coptic mašīr, mešīr, emšīr; also Ar. ʾamšīr. See also ʾamšer, ʾamšīr, mekīr.
from Ar. (ʾal)muštarī, the planet Jupiter; also Amh. māštārī, Syr. muštarī.

meaning: come, happen to, occur to, come upon, arise
origin_explanation: (with the acc) ‘overtake’; (zayəməsṣəʾ ‘future’); ʾamsāya ‘bring, offer, cause to happen, bring about, raise up, convey (a thing to a place), put forth’; tamāsāya ‘bring to one another’; ʾastamāsəʾa ‘adjoin, place one thing on top of another, (T) pull, draw’.

SEMITIC: SAr. mṣy 'go, proceed, march', Heb. māṣā (mṣy) 'find', originally 'reach something', Aram. maṣā 'reach, be able', Syr. maṣā 'be able, have the power, be allowed, find' (Noeldeke, ZDMG 40[1886],736), Md. mṣa 'arrive, reach', Ug. mṣ 'arrive, reach, find', Akk. muṣṣū 'make reach'. See also Delitzsch 158, n.2; J. Blau, IOS 2 (1972),67-72. For Indo-European roots for 'find' reflecting aspects such as 'come upon', see Buck 765. ETHIOPIC: Te. māṣya 'come', Tna. māṣye, Amh.Arg. māṭṭa, māṣṣa, Gur. māṭa.

meaning: 'immerse in water' (rather than 'wring out' as translated in M 119); denominative from moṣāḥ; see waṣḥa.
origin_explanation: denominative from moṣāḥ; see waṣḥa.

meaning: 'beam, wood'; note that Cant. 1:17 has a variant mawāṣaft.
origin_explanation: note that Cant. 1:17 has a variant mawāṣaft.

meaning: 'fade, faint, be emaciated, waste away, wilt, wither, decay, be languid'; 'amaṣlawa, causative; also (K) 'blame, express disapproval, dislike'. (amaṣlawa gaṣṣo 'he had a sad countenance'); tamaṣlawa 'become faded, become emaciated, be of a sad countenance, wrinkle the face'; maṣlaw 'faded, emaciated, wrinkled, withered, failing, near extinction'; maṣlāwe (Gr.117), verbal noun of maṣlawa;

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. māṣlāwā (with metathesis, ṣāmlāwā) 'fade, wither'.

meaning: 'pleurisy, sharp pain caused by a disease'; to be identified with maṣwamo; see ṣa'ama.
origin_explanation: to be identified with maṣwamo; see ṣa'ama.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. māṭamar, māṭabar, same.

meaning: 'rug'; metathesis of maṣfaṭ; see naṣafa I.
origin_explanation: metathesis of maṣfaṭ; see naṣafa I.

meaning: 'masticate, chew, (Gr.117) scratch'; (note that Amh. allammāṭā [translation of maṣṣara] means 'chew' and not 'smile' as given in H 230); 'amaṣṣara (Gr.117) 'cause to scratch'; tamaṣṣara (Gr.117) 'be scratched'; maṣṣāri (Gr.117), act. part. of maṣṣara; maṣṣur (Gr.117), pass. part. of maṣṣara; maṣṣāre (Gr.117), verbal noun of maṣṣara.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. māčra 'chew', Amh. moččārā, m'aččārā, monāččārā 'scratch'. It is possible to connect it with maṣara II in the sense that chewing is an action of spreading out, stretching. See also mṭr.
SEMITIC: Ar. (ta)maṣṣara 'disperse (people)'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. mānāččārā 'strew, scatter about, disperse'. See also maṣara I.

SEMITIC: Ar. maṣṣa 'suck', Mh. meṣ, Šh. miṣṣ, Heb. māṣaṣ, Aram. maṣṣaṣ, Syr. meṣ (mṣṣ), Md. muṣ (mṣṣ), Ug. mṣṣ, mṣw; related to mṣw, mṣy: Ar. maṣṣā 'empty to the last drop' (Dozy), Heb. māṣā 'drain out', Aram. maṣī 'wring', Syr. maṣā 'suck, draw out', Md. mṣa;

meaning: (K) 'kind of animal (in Hebrew taḥaš), (T.K.M) blue or dark (referring to skin)',
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. māṭate 'small, bad-tempered animal'.

meaning: 'turn acid'; a secondary form of mḍd, above.
origin_explanation: a secondary form of mḍd, above.

meaning: 'give alms' (denominative) tamaṣwata 'accept alms'; maṣwāṭi (Gr.118), act. part. of maṣwata; maṣwāt 'almsgiving, alms, charity'; (muḍāya maṣwāt 'treasury');
origin_explanation: from the Aramaic plural miṣwātā (so Dillmann 228, Noeldekke 1910:36, Polotsky 6; but it could also come from the Aramaic singular miṣwātā regardless of the difference in the vocalization); also in Tna.Amh. maṣwāt 'alms'. Note that Gur. maṣwāt 'sacrifice' is to be identified with G. maṣwāt from ṣawa 'sacrifice' rather than with Amh. maṣwāt 'alms' as suggested in Leslau 1979:432. The root passed into Cushitic: Qem. māṭwāt 'charity'.

SEMITIC: Heb. mt, in mōṭ-im 'men', and in proper names, such as maṭūšelaḥ, Ug. mt 'husband', Akk. mutu. See also Noeldekke 1910:146. ETHIOPIC: Te. mit 'husband' (quoted from Noeldekke 1910:146, n.2). Hintze 86, no. 476 connects it with Sid. muta 'male genitalia'.
From Gk. mathētēs 'disciple'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. maṭfāy 'penis' (from Geez). See also noṭfāy.

meaning: 'be deceptive in appearance, appear as a specter'; 'amṭaha (Gr.94) 'look like a specter, consider as a specter'; tamaṭha (K) 'appear as a specter'; maṭāḥi 'who appears as a specter, who is endowed with the evil eye'; maṭuḥ (K*), same; maṭha, see maṭhat;
'phantom, fantasy, specter, ghost, illusion, imagination, magic, black magic'; (ba'abā maṭhat 'actors'); (ba'alā maṭhat 'sorcerer'); maṭhatāwi 'imaginary, actor';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. maṭha 'go astray, err', tamaṭṭaha 'be confused' (Dillmann 183). Praetorius 1890:22 considers Ar. mṭh a denominative from maṭha going back to tāha (twh) 'err'. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. maṭhat 'magic, spell, specter, apparition', Tna. maṭhat, Amh. maṭhat, maṭat; probably also Gur. m'aṭāṭā 'convince by coaxing < do something by magic'. See also mṭh.

meaning: 'appear as a specter, appear in disguise';
origin_explanation: denominative from maṭhat; see maṭha, above.

meaning: 'sword'; in the context given by K 615 it could also be for maṭḥ 'phantom, illusion' (for which see maṭha).
origin_explanation: in the context given by K 615 it could also be for maṭḥ 'phantom, illusion' (for which see maṭha).

meaning: Amh. ṣaṣṣa 1) 'be thin', 2) 'be greedy, be insatiable, be avaricious'; maṭha (T) = Amh. ṣaṣ, rāqiq 'thin'; maṭhat (Gr.94) 'avarice, voracity';
origin_explanation: inasmuch as Amh. ṣaṣṣa is ambiguous, it is probably safe to rely on the translation 'greedy, voracious' given by Ludolf 78 and Gr.94; however, voracity is Tayyā's translation of maṭha, would indicate that ṣaṣ (likewise given by Tayyā) also means 'thin'.

meaning: maṣi'i, act. part. of maṣwa; maṣe (K*) 'one who comes'; maṣi'at 'coming, arrival, advent'; 'amṣa'i, part. of 'amṣa'a; mamṣa', mamṣa'i, same; also 'one who helps to bring'; mamṣa'a, mamṣa' 'place of origin'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: SaR. mṣ' 'go, proceed, march', Heb. māṣā (mṣ') 'find', originally 'reach something', Aram. maṣā 'reach, be able', Syr. maṣā 'be able, have the power, be allowed, find' (Noeldeke, ZDMG 40[1886],736), Md. mṣa 'arrive, reach', Ug. mṣ' 'arrive, reach, find', Akk. muṣṣu 'make reach'. See also Delitzsch 158, n.2; J. Blau, IOS 2 (1972),67-72. For Indo-European roots for 'find' reflecting aspects such as 'come upon', see Buck 765. ETHIOPIC: Te. māṣa 'come', Tna. māṣe, Amh.Arg. māṭṭa, maṣṣa, Gur. māṭa.

origin_explanation: denominative from moṣāh; see waṣha.
note that Cant. 1:17 has a variant mawāṣaft.

phonetics: maṣlawa 'fade, faint, be emaciated, waste away, wilt, wither, decay, be languid'
meaning: maṣlawa 'fade, faint, be emaciated, waste away, wilt, wither, decay, be languid'; 'amaṣlawa, causative; also (K) 'blame, express disapproval, dislike'; ('amaṣlawa gaṣṣo 'he had a sad countenance'); tamaṣlawa 'become faded, become emaciated, be of a sad countenance, wrinkle the face'; maṣlaw 'faded, emaciated, wrinkled, withered, failing, near extinction'; maṣlāwe (Gr.117), verbal noun of maṣlawa
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. māṣläwä (with metathesis, ṣämläwä) 'fade, wither'.
see maṭlawz.

phonetics: maṣāmo (T*) 'pleurisy, sharp pain caused by a disease'
meaning: maṣāmo (T*) 'pleurisy, sharp pain caused by a disease'
origin_explanation: to be identified with maṣamo; see ṣa'ama.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. māṭamar, māṭabär, same.
metathesis of maṣanfät; see naṣafa I.

phonetics: maṣara I, maṣṣara , maddara 'masticate, chew, (Gr.117) scratch'
meaning: maṣara I, maṣṣara , maddara 'masticate, chew, (Gr.117) scratch' (note that Amh. allammāṭṭā [translation of maṣṣara] means 'chew' and not 'smile' as given in H 230); 'amaṣṣara (Gr.117) 'cause to scratch'; tamaṣṣara (Gr.117) 'be scratched'; maṣṣāri (Gr.117), act. part. of maṣṣara; maṣṣur (Gr.117), pass. part. of maṣṣara; maṣṣāre (Gr.117), verbal noun of maṣṣara.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. māčrä 'chew', Amh. mäččärä, m'aččärä, monāččärä 'scratch'. It is possible to connect it with maṣara II in the sense that chewing is an action of spreading out, stretching. See also mṭr.
SEMITIC: Ar. (ta)maṣṣara 'disperse (people)'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. mänāččärä 'strew, scatter about, disperse'. See also maṣara I.
see ṣarya.

SEMITIC: Ar. maṣṣa 'suck', Mh. mṣṣ, Heb. māṣaṣ, Aram. mṣaṣ, Syr. mṣṣ (mṣṣ), Md. mṣṣ (mṣṣ), Ug. mṣṣ, mṣw; related to msw, mṣy: Ar. maṣṣā 'empty to the last drop' (Dozy), Heb. māṣā 'drain out', Aram. maṣi 'wring', Syr. maṣṣā 'suck, draw out', Md. mṣa

phonetics: maṣit, maṣesit, maṣesit (Lt.T.M) 'kind of animal (in Hebrew taḥaš), (T.K.M) blue or dark (referring to skin), blue skin'
meaning: maṣit, maṣesit, maṣesit (Lt.T.M) 'kind of animal (in Hebrew taḥaš), (T.K.M) blue or dark (referring to skin), blue skin'
origin_explanation: and related to mws, mṣy: Heb. mṣṣ 'the pressing out (of milk)', PBh. mṣṣ 'sap', Aram. māyeṣ 'who sucks'. The root is also related to mzz: Ar. mazz 'suck', Mh. muzz, Akk. mazzū II, mazzū II 'press out'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. māṭṭāṭā 'suck', māṭṭāmāṭā, Tna. maṣāyā, māṭmāṭā, Te. māṣiṣ 'juice, sap'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. māṭaṭe 'small, bad-tempered animal'.
a secondary form of mḍd, above.

meaning: maṣwata 'give alms' (denominative); tamaṣwata 'accept alms'; maṣwā't (Gr.118), act. part. of maṣwata; maṣwāt 'almsgiving, alms, charity'; (muḍāya maṣwāt 'treasury')
origin_explanation: from the Aramaic plural miṣwātā (so Dillmann 228, Noeldekke 1910:36, Polotsky 6; but it could also come from the Aramaic singular miṣwātā regardless of the difference in the vocalization); also in Tna.Amh. maṣwāt 'alms'. Note that Gur. maṣwāt 'sacrifice' is to be identified with G. maṣwāt from ṣawa 'sacrifice' rather than with Amh. maṣwāt 'alms' as suggested in Leslau 1979:432. The root passed into Cushitic: Qem. nādwāt 'charity'.

SEMITIC: Heb. mt, in mət-im 'men', and in proper names, such as mətūšelaḥ, Ug. mt 'husband', Akk. mūtu. See also Noeldekke 1910:146. ETHIOPIC: Te. mit 'husband' (quoted from Noeldekke 1910:146, n.2). Hintze 86, no. 476 connects it with Sid. mutā 'male genitalia'.
from Gk. mathētēs 'disciple'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. maṭay 'penis' (from Geez). See also nōṭay.

phonetics: maṭha (yəmṭəḥ) (Y) 'deceptive in appearance, appear as a specter'
meaning: maṭha (yəmṭəḥ) (Y) 'deceptive in appearance, appear as a specter'; 'amṭaha (Gr.94) 'look like a specter, consider as a specter'; tamaṭha (K) 'appear as a specter'; maṭāḥi 'who appears as a specter, who is endowed with the evil eye'; maṭuḥ (K*), same; maṭhat, see maṭhat; maṭhat (T) 'phantom, fantasy, specter, ghost, illusion, imagination, magic, black magic'; (ṣab'a maṭhat 'actors'); (ba'āla maṭhat 'sorcerer'); maṭhatāwi 'imaginary, actor'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. maṭha 'go astray, err', tamaṭṭaha 'be confused' (Dillmann 183). Praetorius 1890:22 considers Ar. mṭḥ a denominative from maṭha going back to tāḥa (twh) 'err'. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. maṭhat 'magic, spell, specter, apparition', Tna. maṭhat, Amh. maṭhat, məṭat; probably also Gur. m'aṭāṭā 'convince by coaxing < do something by magic'. See also miṭhi.

phonetics: mṭht, tamaṭatha 'appear as a specter, appear in disguise'
meaning: mṭht, tamaṭatha 'appear as a specter, appear in disguise'
origin_explanation: denominative from maṭhat; see maṭha, above.
in the context given by K 615 it could also be for maṭh 'phantom, illusion' (for which see maṭha).

meaning: maṭha (L.T.MA), maṭha (K.M) = Amh. ṣaṣṣa 1) 'be thin', 2) 'be greedy, be insatiable, be avaricious'; maṭh (T) = Amh. ṣaṣ, rāqiq 'thin'; maṭhat (Gr.94) 'avarice, voracity'.
origin_explanation: inasmuch as Amh. ṣaṣṣa is ambiguous, it is probably safe to rely on the translation 'greedy, voracious' given by Ludolf 78 and Gr.94; however, Gr.94, Tayyä's translation of maṭh, would indicate that ṣaṣ (likewise given by Tayyä) also means 'thin'.

SEMITIC: metathesis in relation to Kif. Ar. katif, kif 'shoulder'. Sh. ketf 'back of shoulder', Heb. kâtep 'shoulder', Aram. Syr. kitpā, Md. kadpa, Ug. ktf. Akk. katappatu 'part of ribs'. ETHIOPIC: Te. mäktaf.

meaning: (Gr.95) mā-tān 'sinew, nerve, muscle'; 'strings of a musical instrument'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. matn 'half or side of the back', Soq. moten 'hip', Heb. moṭn-ayim, Aram. maṭnayyā, Syr. maṭnāṭā 'loin', Md. matna, Ug. mṭn 'nerve' (Caquot 431), Akk. matnu 'sinew'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. mātnī 'nerve, sinew', Amh. māṭən 'sinew' (from Geez).

meaning: 'be sweet'; matuq (K) 'sweet, tasty'; matqat (K) 'sweetness'; see matuq.

'cut, cut off, cut up, cut asunder, break up, interrupt, shut off, de-pose, remove (from office), excommunicate, exterminate, decree, decide, determine'; mattara, same (intensive); 'amtara, caus. of matara; tamatra, pass. of matara; matāri, act. part. of matara; also 'decisive'; matur 'cut, cut off, amputated piece, deposed, removed, decided, decisive, decreed, absolute, strict (commandment), stern (judgment), se-vere'; (matara 'precisely, certainly, absolutely, deci-sively'); mattār 'piece, a cut piece, strip, fragment, segment'; matrāf, verbal noun of matara; mamtart 'cleft, segment, piece';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. matara 'cut'; related to bṭr: Ar. batara, Heb. bāṭar. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Gur. māṭarā 'cut', Amh. māṭṭarā, Te. māṭra 'grab up, clear (a field)', māṭar 'field of a broken ground'.

meaning: (T), metrāyqəy (K), name of a body of troops; see Dillmann 1139, under gälläl.
(T) 'who destroys'; misprint for maṭfāf, maṭfə'i; see ṭaf'a.
'veil, (T.TS) wrap up, put on clothes'; 'amoṭṭaha 'veil clothe'; tamoṭṭaha 'veil oneself, cover oneself'; muṭṭāhe 'act of veiling, act of covering'; denominative from moṭāht, for which see waṭṭaha.

origin_explanation: metathesis of mawṭalz.
Dillmann 1219 derives it, with reservations, from tam'a, below.

meaning: 'it is seemly'; maṭṭana 'measure, measure out, estimate'; 'amaṭṭana (Gr.116), causative; tamaṭṭana, passive; maṭṭāni, act. part. of maṭṭana; maṭṭun 'measured'; maṭan (pl. 'amtān) 'measure, measurement, ex-tent, circumference, amount, dimension, propor-tion, value, worth, quantity, size, duration, moderation'; matan- with suffix pronouns 'it is enough for, (he) deserving of, (he) worthy of'; (maṭana 'akāl 'manhood'); (za'alboṭṭu maṭana 'boundless, endless'); maṭana (construct state and the accusative used adverbially) 'during, according to, about, at a distance (of), as long as, as large as, as often as, for the space of (a certain time), of the size of, as much as, as great as, so great, so much a degree'; (maṭana za ('ənta) 'such a, so many, so much as this, as much as, so great, in proportion'); (bə-maṭana-a 'for the space of, in propor-tion to'); (hakama maṭana + noun 'according to, in proportion to'); (zə-maṭana 'as great as'); (bə-maṭana 'not as much as'); (ka-maṭana 'how, like'); (kamaṭana za 'such, such a, so great a'); (nī-maṭan, mī-maṭana 'how much?, how more?, how great?, how large?, how how many?, how often?, how much, how great!'); ('amna mi-maṭana 'however much'); 'amtān (pl. of maṭan) 'extent, dimension, size, duration'; 'amṭāna, ba-'amṭāna 'as long as, as large as, as often as, as many as, as much as, in accordance with, according to, in proportion to, to the extent of, in regard to, like, since'; (basuha 'amtān 'who is of mature age'); maṭṭāne 'measure, amount, extension, quantity, dimension, valuation, estimation, analogy'; maṭṭano (t) 'estimate'; maṇṭanṭin 'petty, very small, mediocre, insignificant';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Praetorius, ZDMG 62 (1908).751-2 con-nects it with Ar. (Hadr. Dat.) tayn 'measure', tayn 'according to'), possibly from a combi-nation becoming mtn. This derivation is not convincing. ETHIOPIC: Tna. māṭānā 'measure', Amh. māṭṭānā 'measure out the right amount', Tna.Amh.Gur. māṭān 'amount, dimension'. Littmann-Höfner 143 compare Te. māṭṭana 'to complete'; would it mean 'to complete the measure?' The root passed into Cushitic. Bil. maṭān 'measure, cause'.
from mṭn with reduplication of the last two radicals, and insertion of n; see maṭana.

meaning: 'cut, tear'; instead of maṭanaya of Y.

'lift up, raise, elevate, (T.Y.M) be high, be long'; (Amh. awāṭṭa, translation of maṭṭaqa, means 'here raise, make ascend', not 'drive out' as in H 575); 'amṭaqa 'raise, elevate'; tamaṭaqa, pass. of maṭaqa; maṭāqi (Gr.115) act. part. of maṭaqa; maṭuq 'high, raised'; maṭiq (Gr.115) maṭq 'height'; maṭqat (Gr.450) 'height'. mamṭaqi (K*) act. part. of 'amṭaqa;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. māṭṭāqā 'rise up, fly'.

meaning: 'snatch away'; tamaṭaqa (K*Gr.115), passive; maṭāqi (K*Gr.115) 'who snatches away'; maṭuq (Gr.115) 'snatched away'; maṭqat (Gr.116) 'snatching away' (n);
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. māččäqa 'eat up, end', Amh. mānaṭ-ṭāqā 'snatch away'.

SEMITIC: appears as mtq and mṭq: Heb. māṭaq 'be sweet', Aram. Syr. maṭaq, Ug. mtq 'sweet', and Ar. maṭaqa 'sweetness', Soq. meṭoq 'sweetened'. C. Rabin, Orientalia 32 (1963). 130 (following Laroche) sus-pects a Hittite origin for this root. ETHIOPIC: Te. māṭṭāqa 'be sweet'. For Geez, see also mataqa, above.
is a denominative from *maṭqal, from ṭaqala, also in Amh. māṭqəl 'a kind of sorcerer'.

meaning: 'mark, goal, aim'; transcription of Heb. maṭṭārā, same.
origin_explanation: transcription of Heb. maṭṭārā, same.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Te. maṭarär 'spine' (in Reinisch 1887:278).
from Gk. mirā 'headband'. See also meqri.
from Gk. mēṭropolitēs 'metropolitan bishop'. through Chr.-Ar. maṭrabaliṭ / also Syr. meṭropolitā. See also muṭrān.
from Chr.-Ar. muṭrān < Syr. miṭrān; also Amh. maṭrān. Graf 106 derives it from Gk. mēṭropolitēs 'metropolitan bishop'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. m°aṭṭaṭa 'wipe clean (sauce), consume, finish'; also māṭṭāṭā 'suck clean (e.g. finger dipped in sauce)'. Tna. māṭṭāṭā, māṭmāṭā 'suck'. See also m°aṭaṭa.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. m°aṭṭāṭā 'scrape, scrape clean, scratch'. See also maṭaṭa.

meaning: 'hand over, deliver, give over, grant, surrender, consign, present, transmit'; ṭamattawa, passive; also 'receive, accept, partake of, take, take hold of'; maṭṭāwī, act. part. of maṭṭawa; maṭṭaw, pass. part. of maṭṭawa; maṭṭāwe (K*) 'giving over (n), delivery, consignment'; maṭṭaṭawī (Gr.117), verbal noun of maṭṭawa; mamattaw (Lt) 'traitor'; ṭamattawī, part of ṭamattawa.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ('a)nṭā 'give' derived from 'amṭā (mṭy) according to Noeldeke, ZDMG 40 (1886), 736, n.5 (but see Landberg 1942:2786 who considers it a contamination of 'aṭā and 'anḍā 'bring') related to Soq. miy 'attain', Aram.-Syr. mōṭā 'attain, reach', (pa'el) 'bring', Md. mṭa 'reach, attain', for which see mas°a. J. Blau, IOS 2 (1972), 68, n. 3 disagrees with Noeldeke's interpretation, and ib. pp. 68, 72 he connects G. maṭṭawa with Ar. mṭw 'stretch, draw, walk' (see n. 3), and SAr. mṭw 'walk, march'.

meaning: 'conquer, vanquish, overcome, prevail, defeat, subdue, surpass in power'; 'amō'a, 'amō'a, causative; also 'give victory, let gain victory'; ṭamawwa, ṭamō'a, pass. of mo'a; mawwā'ī (pl. mawwā'əyān), act. part. of mo'a; also 'victor, victorious, conqueror'; mawwəw, mawwəwə (fem. mwə'ət, Gr.105), pass. part. of mo'a; mawī' (Lt) 'power, dominion'; mwə'at 'victory, conquering (n)'; mawwā'ə (K*) 'means of winning'; məwwā'e 'victory, song of triumph'; ṭamawwā'ī 'conquered, vanquished'; ṭamwə't, ṭamwə't 'overcoming (n), conquering'; ṭamawwə'o (t) 'defeat'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the identification with Akk. mu'u 'be willing' (variant of mû according to CAD 10, I, p.435) suggested by von Soden 665 is doubtful. Likewise the connection with Ar. mā'a (my') 'run (horse)' suggested by Hommel 75 is unlikely since the Geez root is mw' ፟ (with ') even though it is occasionally spelled ፟ (with r). Note that the meaning of Akk. mû 'be mighty, surpass' given in Leslau, JAOS 64 (1944), 56 is not recorded in Akkadian dictionaries. ETHIOPIC: common with Cushitic, represented in Or. mo'a 'rule, conquer, win', Sid. mō, Ka. maw.

meaning: see ma'alṭ; (ba'āla mawā'əl, title of the one who keeps the king company).
'large earthenware vessel for making butter'; it has the appearance of a plural.

origin_explanation: denominative from mofī, from wfy.

meaning: 'grow hot, become intense (heat)'; 'amōqa 'heat, warm'; ṭamawwaqa 'warm oneself, warm one another'; 'astamawwaqa 'warm, keep warm, heat, cause to become hot, reheat'; moq 'warmth, heat'; mawwaq, mawwuq 'warm, hot, burning, fervent'; muqaṭ 'heat, warmth, fervor'; 'amwāqī (Gr.104) 'who heats'; mamwāq 'place where one heats (something) or keeps warm'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. Gaf. moqā 'be hot', Tna. moqā, Te.Arg. moqa, Har. mōqa, Gur. m°aqā.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. mawqe 'disease that causes distension of the abdomen'. On the variants of this noun, see Streleyn 1955:328. See also māq°ā.
denominative from *mawarh, metathesis of marawh, above.
metathesis of marāwəḥt; see (rwh I), marwāḥt.

meaning: 'scrub, pick (the teeth), polish by rubbing, wash'; ṭamoṣa, ṭamawṣa (K*), ṭamawwəḍa (T), passive; mawwāṣī (K*), act. part. of moṣa; mawwāṣ (K*), pass. part. of moṣa.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. māṣa (mws) 'wash, scrub'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. māwwāṣā 'pick the teeth, brush the teeth', Te. (ṭa)māyəṣa, Amh. m°aṣṣa. See also moya-ḍa.

meaning: (T) 'compensate, repair, make amends, pay damages'; 'amoṣa (K*), causative; ṭamawṣa, ṭamawṣa (K*.T.M) 'compensate one another, be reconciled with one another'; 'astamawwaṣa 'reconcile'; mawwāṣī (K*), act. part. of moṣa; mawwəṣ (K*) 'compensated'; moṣā (K*), moḍā (T) 'compensation'.
origin_explanation: denominative from moṣā, moḍā, for which see waḍ°a.

meaning: 'wooden walls, beams'; variant of mawṣəḥt, for which see moṣāḥt, above.
pl. of moṣāḥt, above. Note that Cant. 1:17 also has the variant mawāḍəft.

'die'; (zəyəmawmuṭ 'mortal'); 'amōṭa, 'amaṭa 'let die, put to death, kill, have killed'; mawwāṭī 'mortal, dead'; ('imawwāṭī 'immortal'); mawwəṭ, mawwuṭ (fem. məwwəṭṭ, pl. maṣc. məwwəṭān, muṭān) 'dead, deceased'; (bāḥr məwwəṭ 'the Dead Sea'); moṭ 'death, destruction'; (ṣā'ra moṭ 'agony'); muṭat 'death, dying (n)'; *mawīṭ, 'imawīṭ 'immortality'.
'place where one dies'; 'corpse, carcass, dead body'; '(ag°āla māwātā 'orphan'); 'orphanhood'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. māta (mwt) 'die', SAr. mwt, Mh. mōt, Heb. mēt, Aram.Syr. mītā, mīt, Md. mut, Ug. mt, Akk. mātu. ETHIOPIC: motā, mota.

meaning: (T.D 1397, under waṭlata) 'chest strap for a mount'; see wīlt.
(T) 'knife'; while the root is waṭlaza 'cut' (see below), cp. also Amh. māṭallāzā, māzallāṭā 'draw swords on one another'. See also maṭlawz.
(T.Sw. in D 1394) 'object received or taken from someone'; probably a misprint for məṭāw, from maṭṭawa.

(pl. māyāt) 'water, liquid'; '(māya ʾaskāl 'wine'); '(māya ʾayḥ 'deluge'); '(māya ḥalīb 'whey'); '(māya ḥəmmāt 'ink'); '(māya ləbn 'stacte'); '(māya ṣalōt 'holy water'); māyāwī 'related to water'; tamaya (Gr.) 'change into water' (denominative)
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. māʾ 'water', SAr. mw, Hars. he-myōḥ, Heb. may-im, Aram. Syr. mayyā, Md. mai, Ug. my, Akk. māʾu, mī, māmū, māwū. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Gur. may 'water', Har. mī, mūy, Amh. may 'miraculous water' (from Geez). For this root in Hamito-Semitic (Afroasiatic), see Cohen 1947: no. 485.
SEMITIC: Onomatopoetic; Ar. māʾ (mwʾ) 'mew (cat)'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. maʾ bālā 'bleat (goat)', Amh. meʾalā.
SEMITIC: Ar. māʾ (mwʾ) 'flow (liquid)', (ta)mayyā 'melt' (intr). See also maʾawa.
tamayba (Gr.111), passive; mayābī (Gr.111), act. part. of meba; mayyub (Gr.111), pass. part. of meba; mibat (Gr.111) 'placing (n) on the fire'.

meaning: (T.Y) 'clean (the teeth), pick the teeth, brush the teeth'; see also mwṣ I.

(K*); see tamayyana tamayyana 'deceive, deal craftily, beguile, falsify, cheat, corrupt, do slyly, be shrewd, be treacherous, use skill'; ʾastamayyana (K*), causative; mayyūn, mayyən 'malicious, who devises evil, crafty, shrewd, sly, (K*) deceived, cheated'; min 'cunning, fraud, trickery, ruse'; mayyāne, (L.T) miyyāne 'deception, delusion, deceiving, treachery'; tamayyānī, part. of tamayyana; also 'deceitful, false'; tamayyano (t) 'astuteness, fraud, pretense, cunning behavior, guile, deceitfulness'; tamyān 'fraud, deceit, deceitfulness, falseness'; tamyānt 'craftiness, ruse, trickery, deceit, guile, cunning, cheating, malice, wickedness, stratagem'; tamyāntāwī (Gr.449) 'fraudulent';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. mānā (myn) 'tell a lie'. A.M. Honeyman, VT 5 (1955). 220 gives the root mwn, myn the meaning 'split' > 1) 'divide, plow'; 2) 'dissemble, act with duplicity, lie, counterfeit', developed in Hebrew into təmūnā 'form, representation' (see also Koehler 519, under *min). H. Rabin, SH 8 (1961). 393 compares it with Mishnaic Hebrew min 'sectarian'. ETHIOPIC: related to Te. (mny), mānī 'falsehood', mānay 'liar', from mny 'create' (for 'c' that is, 'invent something', cp. faṭara, above).

meaning: (T) 'serve mead at a banquet' (denominative from mes);
origin_explanation: mayyasa (T.M) 'prepare mead, brew mead'; ʾamesa, same; (K*) also causative; tamaysa (K), pass. of mesa; mayyāsī 'one who prepares mead, one who sells mead'; mayyus (K*), pass. part. of mayyasa; mes (pl. ʾamyās) 'drink made from fermented honey, mead'; (mes za-wayn [Lt] 'wine'); masyān (T.M) 'drink made from ferney, mead'; SEMITIC: Ar. māta (mwt) 'mix well', SAr. mwt (?) (W. Müller 1983:281). ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. mes 'honeyed water', māyāṣā 'prepare mead', Te. māyāṣā; ʾic: Af. mēs 'mead', Sa. mēz, Qem. mīz (with alternance s:z:d).
ETHIOPIC: Amh. (tā)mayyāsā 'retreat, go back'.

meaning: (yəmit) 'turn, turn aside (tr., intr), turn away, turn back, divert, convert, transform, alter, change, restore, return, repel, frustrate, revoke, refute, pervert, deliver up, recall';
origin_explanation: ʾameṭa (K*), causative; also 'take back'; tamayṭa, tameṭa, passive, reflexive; also 'face about, return (intr), go back, turn in, to turn back, turn away (intr)'; (zəʾiyyətmayyaṭ 'immutable, unwavering'); tamayṭayata 'turn hither and thither' (Amharic form); mayyāṭī, act. part. of meṭa; mayṭāt 'conversion'; mayyut, pass. part. of meṭa; also 'apostate'; mayyāṭ (T) 'spindle (that is, 'something that turns')'; mayyāṭe, muyāṭe 'alteration, transformation, confusion, change, changing, revolution (of stars)'; mayṭot 'conversion'; miṭāt (pl. miṭatāt) 'turning (n), turn, turning, returning, return, answer, change, transformation, conversion, revolution (going around)'; miṭān 'returning (n), conversion, alteration, answer'.

tamyāṭī, part. of tamayṭa; tamayṭo (t) 'turning back (n), conversion, reduction, restoration'; mastamayāṭ 'one who is given to changing his mind, inconsistent'; SEMITIC: Ar. māṭa (myṭ) 'turn away, retire from, deviate', Heb. māṭ 'totter', PbH. myṭ (ḥif-il) 'bend, shake', Aram.Syr. māṭ 'incline, waver, decline'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. māyāṭā 'turn over (grain)'.

meaning: eighth Ethiopian month (from 9 April to 8 May);
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. Amh. miyazaya. Conti Rossini 1925:495-6 explains it through Ar. māza (myz) 'go from one place to another', that is, "Il miyāzayā dunque sarebbe il tempo nel quale le popolazioni, rimaste per pascoli e per coltivazione nella zona costiera migrano sul l'altipiano per goderve colà delle piogge estive".
From Ar. mawz (rarely mūz), of Indic (moca) origin; also Te.Tna.Amh. muz.

meaning: 'lie in ruins, be deserted, become desolate, be destroyed, be demolished, be devastated';
origin_explanation: ʾamazbara 'lay waste, destroy, demolish, ruin, wreck'; tamazbara, pass. of ʾamazbara; mazbārī, act. part. of mazbara; mazbūr 'destroyed, demolished, desolate, devastated, ruined'; mazbar (fem. mazbārt), same; mazbar, mazbār (pl. mazbārāt) 'ruin, desert, desolate place, wilderness'; mazbāre 'destruction, devastation, ruin'; ʾamazbārī (Gr.110), part. of ʾamazbara; ETHIOPIC: Tna. māzbārā 'destroy, devastate, interrupt', Amh. māzbārā 'ravage, devastate'. The verbs are probably denominatives from mazbar going back to zabara, below.

meaning: (T.Sw. in D 1394) 'kind of bag for storing grain';
origin_explanation: either a variant of mazwad or a misprint for mazwad.

meaning: 'tear, pluck out, snatch away, carry away'
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Related to Ar. mazaga 'tear off (a garment)'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. mäzzäga 'pick (cotton)'; related to Amh. mäzzäqä 'pull, take out'.

meaning: 'cause to speak angrily or arrogantly, (K) cause to be envious or jealous'
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Ar. mazaga ('alā) 'incite against'.

meaning: (T) 'be multicoloured, be striped, be changed, be turned over, (D) throw into confusion by disagreement, (K) lack unity, quarrel''
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: possibly a denominative from *mzg°l, from zg°l and to be compared with Amh. zänäg°g°äla (that is, zg°l with enlarged n) 'muddle, confound, perplex', aznägäg°äla 'turn upside down, interlace, copy a manuscript inserting red letters or words in the otherwise black text'.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. (a)mäsgänä 'thank', Amh. (a)-mäsgäggänä (Cohen 1939:XXV records Amh. tämäs-gän as being pronounced with a voiced s, a pronun-ciation that would explain G. amazgana), Arg. (a)mäsgäggäna; perhaps to be connected with Te. mäzgänä 'be heavy', mzgun 'heavy, dignified'; a causative amazgänä would then mean 'dignify someone > praise, reward, remunerate, thank' (see also mäzgänä below). For the root 'be heavy' devel-oped into 'honor, dignify', cp. Heb. kābēd 'be heavy', kibbēd 'honor, glorify'. The root passed into Cushit-ic. Bil. mäsgän 'be honored', Qua. mesgan, Qem. mäsgänä 'glory, praise'. See also mäzgänä, below.
variant of bäzgänä, above, with alternance of the labials m:b.
ETHIOPIC: Te. mäzgänä 'be heavy'. See also mzgn, above.

meaning: 'laugh at, jeer at, (K*) dally (with women), be pampered'
origin_explanation: SEMETIC: Ar. mazha (with h) 'joke, jest'. ETHIOPIC: Te. (ta)mäzzäha 'become weak, break down'. See also manzaḥa.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. azha mazha, same (probably from Geez).
SEMETIC: Rabin, EI 9 (1965). 155 identifies it with Heb. sāḥōr 'dark'.

meaning: 'pliant twig (note that the quoted passage of Num. 13:23 does not warrant the meaning 'pliant twig'), pole which two men carry on their shoulders in order to transport a heavy object, long staff, stave, (K*) sack of woven palm leaves in which carpenters and black-smiths carry their tools; beehive made of a basket'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. məzlal 'bat, carrying pole'.
to be identified with masmar 'line' (see śmr); for the alternance z:s, see Dillmann 1907:62.

meaning: 'stroke, rub, smear, wipe off, wipe away, (L) dry'
origin_explanation: (mazmaza q°ärbäna 'rub the bread of the Eu-charist to test the soundness of the loaf and to remove loose particles' (Brightman 587); tamazmaza, passive; also 'dry (intr), be dried up, (Lt) perfume' (rather, 'be rubbed with perfume'); mazmuz, pass. part. of mazmaza; mazmazo (T) 'rubbing, stroking (n)' SEMITIC: Ar. mazmaza 'agitate'. ETHIOPIC: Te. mäzmäza 'massage' (re-root in relation to Tna. mäzäwä 'rub (with perfume, oil)', mäzmäzä 'rub (with perfume, oil)', m°azzä 'smear, wipe'; also in Cushitic: Qua. mäz 'wipe off', Bil. mägmäg 'rinse, wash off'.

the verb is a denominative from mīzān borrowed from Ar. mīzān 'scales, balance', from wazana. ETHIOPIC: Te. mäzzäna, mezzäna 'weigh', Tna. mäzänä, Amh.Gur. mäzzänä, Arg. Har. mēzäna; for 'scales, balance', cp. Te.Tna.Amh. mizan.

SEMITIC: in view of SAr. mzr-m (d-tmr-m) 'date wine' it is doubtful whether Ar. mizr 'beverage made from millet, barley, or other grain' is to be considered a Geez loanword, as suggested by Noel-deke 1910:57 (followed by Ullendorff 1955:184). Conti Rossini 1912:180 identifies G. mæzr with Amh. bərz 'honeyed water'. See also masr, above.
Dillmann 1047 (followed by Noel-deke 1910:61) de-rives it from *maśra (see śarä, below) > masra > mazra'.

meaning: 'arm, shoulder (of an animal), sleeve (of a garment), strength'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. dirāʿ 'forearm', Soq. dire, Heb. zərōʿ, ʿəzrōʿ, Aram.Syr. dərāʿā, Md. dra, Ug. dr, Akk. durāʿu 'arm, foreleg' is considered a Western Semitic loanword (CAD 3, 190). For G. mazrärät 'strength', cp. Heb. zərōʿōt (pl.) 'arms, forces'. ETHIOPIC: Te. zärāʿ, mäzarärt 'arm, forearm', Tna. mäzraʿt.
transcription of Heb. mazzārōt 'constellation of the zodiac' through Gk. mazurōth; also in Amh. mäzo-rot.
From Ar. mizwad; also Te. mäswäd. See also mazad.
misprint for duzyähu, for which see above.
SEMITIC: Ar. mäzzä (mzz) 'set apart'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. mäzäzä 'pull out', Amh. mäzäzä, mäzämmäzä, Har. mäzäza, mäsäsa.
